Prospective Memory (Remembering TO Remember)
This is the memory of “when I see X on the exam, I need to recall Y.” It’s about TRIGGERS.

























TechniqueHow It HelpsImplementation intentions”WHEN I see [formula name], I will say [explanation].” If-Then format.Trigger-action pairsWrite on a card: “If question has ‘ISO 27035’ → say ‘5 phases: Plan, Identify, Assess, Respond, Learn.’”Exam simulationHave someone ask you questions in random order. Practice the trigger→response path.Keyword bridgesFor each topic, pick ONE trigger word. When you hear that word, your response unspools automatically.

FOR ORAL EXAMS (Viva, Presentations, Discussion-Based)
Oral exams are different. You don’t need to write perfectly. You need to TALK.
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORAL_PREP["ORAL EXAM PREPARATION"]
        SEC["Study by SECTION<br/>not by page"] --> THREE["The 3-SENTENCE RULE<br/>For every topic know:<br/>1. What it is<br/>2. How it works<br/>3. Why it connects"]
        THREE --> WALK["Practice OUT LOUD<br/>while walking.<br/>Record yourself."]
        WALK --> MIRROR["MIRROR TECHNIQUE<br/>Explain to mirror.<br/>Watch your face.<br/>Look calm."]
        MIRROR --> CONCEPT["Don't memorize scripts.<br/>Know CONCEPTS.<br/>Words will come."]
    end

    subgraph ORAL_DURING["DURING THE ORAL EXAM"]
        PAUSE["Pause between sentences.<br/>Silence = thoughtful."]
        STUCK_ORAL["If you don't know:<br/>'Prof, I can reason through it...'<br/>Connect to something you know."]
        FORGOT["If you forget a word:<br/>Describe it simply.<br/>Then say the technical term.<br/>Points for BOTH."]
        BREATHE["BREATHE.<br/>4 sec in, 4 hold, 4 out.<br/>Under the desk. No one sees."]
    end

    ORAL_PREP --> ORAL_DURING

    style THREE fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style CONCEPT fill:#f9ca24,color:#000

How To Prepare For An Oral Exam


Study by section, not by page. The professor asks “Tell me about decision trees.” Not “Tell me about page 47.”


The 3-Sentence Rule. For EVERY topic, know 3 things:

What it is (1 sentence)
How it works (2 sentences)
Why it connects to something else (1 sentence)



Practice out loud while walking. Record yourself on your phone. Listen back. If you cringe, you’ll remember it better next time.


The Mirror Technique. Stand in front of a mirror. Explain a concept. Watch your face. If you look confused, the professor will see it too. Practice until you look calm.


Don’t memorize scripts. Know the CONCEPTS. The words will come. If you memorize exact sentences and forget one, you panic. Concepts don’t have exact words.


During The Oral Exam

Pause between sentences. Silence is not failure. It makes you sound thoughtful.
If you don’t know: “Professor, I don’t have the precise answer but I can reason through it: [connect to something you know].”
If you forget a word: Describe the concept in simple words. Then say “This is called [technical term].” Points for BOTH understanding AND terminology.
Breathe. 4 seconds in, 4 seconds hold, 4 seconds out. Do it under the desk. No one sees.


FOR THEORY SUBJECTS (Heavy Reading, Concepts, Relationships)
Theory = definitions, concepts, relationships, history. Lots of words. No numbers.
flowchart TB
    THEORY["THEORY TOPIC"] --> MAP["MIND MAP<br/>Center bubble = main topic<br/>Lines to related topics<br/>One word per bubble"]
    MAP --> TEACH["TEACH A GHOST<br/>Explain to imaginary student<br/>Out loud. Walking.<br/>Gap found = go back and fill"]
    TEACH --> TABLE["COMPARE/CONTRAST TABLE<br/>Concept A | Concept B<br/>Same? | Different?"]
    TABLE --> STORY["STORY-IFY IT<br/>Turn theory into a narrative<br/>Characters, drama, timeline"]
    STORY --> DONE_T["THEORY UNDERSTOOD"]

    style MAP fill:#45b7d1,color:#fff
    style TEACH fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff



Map it. Draw a bubble in the center of a page. Write the main topic. Draw lines to related topics. One word per bubble. This is a mind map. Your brain sees connections it misses when reading linearly.


Teach a ghost. Explain the theory to an imaginary student. Out loud. Walking. When you can’t explain something, you’ve found a gap. Go back and fill it. This is the single most powerful technique for theory. Teaching forces you to organize knowledge into coherent semantic structures.


Compare and contrast. Theory is about relationships. Make a simple table:

















Concept AConcept BSame?Different?SVMNeural NetBoth classifySVM has global min, NN doesn’t


Story-ify it. Turn the theory into a story with characters and drama:

“In 1969, Minsky proved the perceptron couldn’t do XOR. Everyone gave up on neural networks. This was the first AI winter: funding vanished, researchers abandoned the field. Then in the 1980s, Rumelhart and Hinton figured out multi-layer networks and backpropagation. AI came back from the dead. Then it died again in the 1990s…”

Stories use episodic memory, which is stronger than semantic memory for you.



FOR WRITTEN EXAMS (Essay Questions, Problem Sets, Proofs)
Written exams require you to PRODUCE, not just recognize.

    subgraph PREP_WRITTEN["WRITTEN EXAM PREPARATION"]
        PREDICT["PREDICT QUESTIONS<br/>Past exams + Syllabus<br/>→ 5-10 big topics<br/>→ Write a question for each"]
        FORMULAS_BLANK["BLANK SHEET: Write every formula<br/>from the chapter from memory.<br/>Mark missed ones. Redo tomorrow."]
        BLANK_METHOD["THE BLANK PAGE METHOD<br/>Empty sheet. Write EVERYTHING<br/>you remember about the subject.<br/>Gaps = what to review."]
    end

    subgraph DURING_WRITTEN["DURING THE WRITTEN EXAM"]
        EASY_FIRST["Read ALL questions first.<br/>Mark easy ones. Do those first.<br/>Build confidence."]
        MOVE_ON["If stuck: MOVE ON.<br/>Come back later.<br/>Brain works on it in background."]
        PARTIAL["For proofs: write what you know.<br/>Write what you need to prove.<br/>Connect them. Partial proof = partial marks."]
        CHECK_BACKWARDS["Check your work BACKWARDS.<br/>Last line → first line.<br/>Brain catches errors it misses<br/>reading forward."]
    end

    PREP_WRITTEN --> DURING_WRITTEN

    style BLANK_METHOD fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style CHECK_BACKWARDS fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff

How To Prepare For Written Exams


Predict the questions. Look at past exams. Look at the syllabus. What are the 5-10 big topics? Write a question for each. Answer them in writing. Under timed conditions.


Practice writing formulas from memory. Blank sheet. Write every formula from a chapter. Don’t peek. Then check. Mark the ones you missed. Redo those tomorrow.


The Blank Page Method. Before the exam, take a blank sheet. Write EVERYTHING you remember about the subject. No book. No notes. Just your brain and a pen. The gaps on the page are EXACTLY what you need to review. This is retrieval practice at its most powerful.


During A Written Exam

Read ALL questions first. Mark the easy ones. Do those first. Build confidence.
If stuck on a question, move on. Come back later. Your brain works on it in the background (unconscious pattern recognition).
For math proofs: Write what you know. Write what you need to prove. Try to connect them. Even a partial proof gets partial marks.
Check your work BACKWARDS. Read your answer from the last line to the first. Your brain catches errors it misses reading forward (breaks the pattern-recognition auto-correct).


FOR MATH AND FORMULAS
Formulas are not meant to be read. They are meant to be SAID out loud.
flowchart TB
    FORMULA["SEE A FORMULA"] --> SAY["SAY IT IN WORDS<br/>'y equals w-one x-one<br/>plus w-two x-two plus b'"]
    SAY --> EXPLAIN["EXPLAIN WHAT IT DOES<br/>'It weighs two inputs,<br/>adds them, says yes or no'"]
    EXPLAIN --> EXAMPLE["INVENT A STUPID EXAMPLE<br/>'If sun (x1) AND weekend (x2),<br/>I go out (y=1)'"]
    EXAMPLE --> WRITE5["WRITE IT 5 TIMES<br/>on blank sheet<br/>while saying it aloud"]
    WRITE5 --> THROW["THROW AWAY SHEET<br/>Rewrite WITHOUT looking"]
    THROW --> PLUG["PLUG IN NUMBERS<br/>Use 0 and 1. Calculate by hand.<br/>Change one variable. See what happens."]
    PLUG --> DRAW["DRAW IT<br/>Sketch the function.<br/>Draw the line.<br/>Your spatial memory is weak →<br/>use paper as external spatial memory."]

    style SAY fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style THROW fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff

The Formula Method

Say the formula in words (not symbols): “y equals sign of w-one x-one plus w-two x-two plus b”
Explain what it does: “It takes two things, weighs them, adds them up, and says yes or no.”
Invent a stupid example: “If there’s sun (x1) and it’s weekend (x2), I go out (y=1).”
Write the formula 5 times on a blank sheet while saying it out loud.
Throw away the sheet. Rewrite it without looking.

Never stare at a formula in silence. It won’t enter your brain.
For Understanding What A Formula MEANS (Not Just Memorizing)

Plug in numbers. Pick simple numbers. 0 and 1. Calculate by hand. See what happens. This builds procedural memory for the formula.
Change one variable. What happens if x1 goes from 0 to 10? Does the output change a lot or a little? This builds implicit pattern recognition.
Draw it. If it’s a function, sketch it. If it’s a line, draw the line. Your spatial memory is weak, so use paper as EXTERNAL spatial memory.


FOR PROGRAMMING AND CODE
Don’t read code. EXECUTE it in your head. Be the computer.
flowchart TB
    CODE["PIECE OF CODE (~5 lines)"] --> EXECUTE["BE THE COMPUTER<br/>Paper and pen.<br/>Execute line by line.<br/>Write variable values at each step."]
    EXECUTE --> REWRITE["REWRITE FROM MEMORY<br/>Blank sheet. Write the code.<br/>No looking."]
    REWRITE --> CHECK{"Missing a<br/>parenthesis?"}
    CHECK -->|"Yes"| EXECUTE
    CHECK -->|"No"| BREAK_IT["BREAK IT<br/>Change one line.<br/>Guess what happens.<br/>Run it. Were you right?"]
    BREAK_IT --> BUILD["BUILD SOMETHING TINY<br/>Calculator. To-do list.<br/>20 lines that WORK.<br/>Finishing = dopamine = memory."]

    style EXECUTE fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style BREAK_IT fill:#f9ca24,color:#000
    style BUILD fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff

The Code Method

Take a piece of code. About 5 lines.
Pretend you’re the computer. Paper and pen. Execute line by line. Write down variable values at each step. This builds PROCEDURAL MEMORY.
Then write the code on a blank sheet. Without looking.
If you miss a parenthesis, restart. Never copy and paste. Ever.
Break it. Change one line. Guess what happens. Run it. Were you right? That’s how you learn.

For Learning Programming Languages

Type every example yourself. Don’t download the code. Type it. Make typos. Fix them. That’s learning: your fingers build procedural memory for syntax.
One concept at a time. Today: if statements. Tomorrow: for loops. Don’t mix: your working memory can’t handle it.
Build something tiny. A calculator. A to-do list. Something that works in 20 lines. Finishing gives DOPAMINE. Dopamine helps memory consolidation.


FOR PRACTICAL STEM (Labs, Projects, Experiments)
Practical work is about DOING, not reading about doing. This builds PROCEDURAL MEMORY.
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE_LAB["BEFORE THE LAB"]
        B1["Read procedure ONCE aloud<br/>5 minutes max"] --> B2["Write steps in YOUR words<br/>Numbered. One action per line."]
        B2 --> B3["PREDICT what should happen.<br/>Write your prediction.<br/>Being wrong teaches more<br/>than being right."]
    end

    subgraph DURING_LAB["DURING THE LAB"]
        D1["After each step ask:<br/>'WHY am I doing this?'"] --> D2["Write results IMMEDIATELY.<br/>Don't trust your memory.<br/>Write the number NOW."]
        D2 --> D3["If something goes wrong:<br/>NOTE IT.<br/>Unexpected results = data,<br/>not failure."]
    end

    BEFORE_LAB --> DURING_LAB

    style B3 fill:#f9ca24,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff

Before The Lab/Project Session

Read the procedure once. Out loud. 5 minutes max.
Write the steps in your own words. Numbered list. One action per line.
Predict what should happen. Write your prediction. After the lab, compare. Being wrong teaches you more than being right: it highlights flaws in your semantic model.

During The Lab

Don’t follow the script blindly. After each step, ask: “WHY am I doing this?”
Write results immediately. Don’t trust your memory. Write the number NOW.
If something goes wrong, note it. Unexpected results are not failures. They are data.


THE “TERRIBLE BRAIN DAY” PROTOCOL

For days when brain fog is so thick you can’t even do Step 1.

Not every day is a study day. Some days your brain simply won’t cooperate. Fighting it burns energy and produces nothing. On those days, use the RIGHT level.
flowchart TB
    WAKE["WAKE UP"] --> CHECK{"How do I feel?"}

    CHECK -->|"OK, I can think"| GREEN["GREEN DAY<br/>Full 5-Step Method<br/>25 min study / 5 min break<br/>New material + recall"]
    CHECK -->|"Foggy, tired but functional"| YELLOW["YELLOW DAY<br/>VOICE ONLY<br/>Read aloud. Repeat orally.<br/>NO writing. NO new concepts.<br/>Review old sentences only."]
    CHECK -->|"Can't connect, exhausted"| RED["RED DAY<br/>PASSIVE ONLY<br/>AI voice reads to you.<br/>Follow with eyes.<br/>Don't try to repeat.<br/>Don't feel guilty."]

    GREEN --> GREEN_OUT["Productive day.<br/>Dopamine journal at night."]
    YELLOW --> YELLOW_OUT["Maintenance day.<br/>You kept memories alive.<br/>That counts."]
    RED --> RED_OUT["Survival day.<br/>Passive exposure still works.<br/>Sleep will process it.<br/>Try again tomorrow."]

    style GREEN fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style YELLOW fill:#f9ca24,color:#000
    style RED fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff






























LevelHow You FeelWhat To DoWhy It Still WorksGreenOk, I can thinkFull 5-Step Method. New material + recall schedule.Optimal encoding. All memory systems engaged.YellowFoggy but functionalVOICE ONLY: Read aloud, repeat orally. NO writing. Only review old sentences, no new concepts.Maintains existing memories. Prevents decay. Vocal loop still encodes.RedCan’t connect, exhaustedPASSIVE ONLY: AI voice reads to you. Follow with eyes. Don’t try to repeat. Don’t feel guilty.Passive exposure still activates semantic networks weakly. Sleep will consolidate what little got through.

A red day is NOT a wasted day. It’s a day you did what your brain could handle. Guilt destroys dopamine. Acceptance preserves it.


FOR FOCUS AND BRAIN FOG
Non-negotiable rules:

25 minutes study, 5 minutes break. Timer on your phone. When it rings, stand up. Walk. Drink water. Do NOT look at your phone during the break. Close your eyes.
Water always nearby. One sip every 10 minutes. Dehydration = brain fog. You don’t need to drink a lot. You need to drink often.
Never study right after eating. Wait 30 minutes. Blood goes to your stomach, not your brain.
No caffeine or energy drinks. Anxiety + caffeine = disaster. Drink water or decaf tea.
Study standing up or walking. Your brain works better in motion. Put your book on a high shelf. Stand. Walk while you repeat.
Background noise: If silence makes your mind wander, use brown noise (not music, not white noise). YouTube: “brown noise for studying.” It fills the silence without distracting.


FOR STRESS AND ANXIETY (Before And During Exams)


4-4-4-4 breathing: Inhale 4 seconds, hold 4, exhale 4, wait 4. Repeat 5 times. This works every time. Even during an exam. Your heart rate drops. Your brain gets oxygen.


Don’t study in the last 30 minutes. What you don’t know, you won’t learn at the last minute. The last 30 minutes are for breathing and drinking water.


Enter smiling. Even if it’s fake. Your brain doesn’t know the difference. Smiling lowers cortisol (stress hormone).


Social anxiety during presentations/orals: The professor has seen hundreds of students. They’ve seen worse presentations than yours. They’ve seen people freeze, cry, forget everything. You won’t be the worst. You won’t even be memorable. Just get through it.


Before sleep the night before: No screens for 30 minutes. Read something non-academic. Your brain consolidates memory during sleep. Let it work.



EMERGENCY CRAMMING (When You Have 30 Minutes)

For when you have NO time. This is damage minimization, not ideal learning.


Read ONLY the section titles. One after another. Aloud.
For each title, say ONE sentence about it. If you can’t, read the first line of that section.
Do NOT read the whole text. Your brain in panic mode cannot absorb paragraphs. It CAN absorb titles and first lines.
At the end, close your eyes. List the titles you remember. That’s what you know. The rest was never going to stick in 30 minutes anyway.


LONG-TERM MEMORY: How To Remember SOMETHING FOREVER

This is the difference between “I passed the exam” and “I still know this 5 years later.”

flowchart TB
    LEARN_LT["LEARN IT"] --> WEEK1["WEEK 1: Recall schedule<br/>(1h, 3h, sleep, next day, 3 days)"]
    WEEK1 --> WEEK2["WEEK 2: 2-min review"]
    WEEK2 --> MONTH1["MONTH 1: 2-min review"]
    MONTH1 --> MONTH3["MONTH 3: 1-min review"]
    MONTH3 --> MONTH6["MONTH 6: 1-min review"]
    MONTH6 --> YEAR1["YEAR 1: 30-sec check"]
    YEAR1 --> PERMANENT["PERMANENT<br/>SEMANTIC MEMORY"]

    subgraph DECAY["WITHOUT REVIEW"]
        D1["Day 1: 100%"] --> D2["Day 2: 60%"] --> D3["Day 7: 30%"] --> D4["Month 1: 10%"] --> D5["Year 1: 0%"]
    end

    style PERMANENT fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style DECAY fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff

The Spaced Repetition Principle For Lifetime Retention
Each time you successfully recall something RIGHT BEFORE you were about to forget it, the memory gets STRONGER and lasts LONGER. The intervals expand exponentially:




























































Review #WhenWhat Happens1ImmediateMemory formed (fragile)2+1 hourStrengthened for ~1 day3+3 hoursStrengthened for ~3 days4Before sleepConsolidated overnight5Next dayStrengthened for ~1 week6+3 daysStrengthened for ~2 weeks7+1 weekStrengthened for ~1 month8+1 monthStrengthened for ~3 months9+3 monthsStrengthened for ~1 year10+1 yearEssentially permanent

The first 4 reviews (same day) are the ones that matter MOST. They determine whether something survives to Day 2. After Day 2, the intervals grow and the reviews become trivially short (1-2 minutes each).

The “Big Picture” Annual Review
Once a year, spend 30 minutes doing a “brain dump” of everything you learned that year:

Take a blank sheet. Write every major concept, formula, and method you remember.
Don’t look anything up. This is a test of what SURVIVED.
Whatever is missing: that’s what needs a single 2-minute review.
One 2-minute review per year is enough to keep something in memory for life.


DAILY ROUTINE FOR YOUR BRAIN
flowchart TB
    subgraph MORNING["MORNING"]
        M1["10 min walk / movement<br/>(Boosts BDNF = brain fertilizer)"] --> M2["Hardest subject first<br/>(Brain is fresh)"]
        M2 --> M3["25 min study → 5 min break<br/>Repeat 2-3 cycles"]
    end

    subgraph MIDDAY["MIDDAY"]
        MID1["Moderate subject<br/>(Switch topics to stay engaged)"] --> MID2["25 min study → 5 min break"]
    end

    subgraph AFTERNOON["AFTERNOON"]
        A1["Wait 30 min after lunch"] --> A2["Active work ONLY<br/>(Practice problems, coding, labs)<br/>No new theory"]
    end

    subgraph EVENING["EVENING"]
        E1["Quick review of morning material<br/>(5-10 min per topic, aloud)"] --> E2["Dopamine Journal:<br/>3 things I learned today"]
        E2 --> E3["No screens 30 min before bed<br/>Water. 4-4-4-4 breathing."]
    end

    MORNING --> MIDDAY --> AFTERNOON --> EVENING

    style M1 fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style E1 fill:#f9ca24,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff









































TimeActionMemory System TargetedMorning10 min walk/movement FIRST. Then hardest subject. 25/5 cycles.Working memory at peak. BDNF boost from exercise.Mid-morningModerate subject. Switch topics to keep brain engaged.Interleaving builds implicit pattern recognition.After lunchWait 30 min. Light review, NOT new material.Blood is in stomach, not brain. Don’t fight biology.AfternoonPractice problems, coding, or lab work (active, not passive).Procedural memory. Hands-on encoding.EveningQuick review of morning material (5-10 min per topic, aloud). Dopamine journal.Feeds hippocampus for overnight consolidation.Before bedNo screens. Water. 4-4-4-4 breathing.Sleep quality determines memory retention.

THE COMPLETE SYSTEM: Overview Flowchart
flowchart TB
    PREVIEW["STEP 0: 30-sec Preview<br/>Build mental shelf"] --> PIECE["STEP 1: Take 2-min piece<br/>Cover rest with paper"]
    PIECE --> READ["STEP 2: Read aloud + Simplify<br/>Say it like to a 10-year-old"]
    READ --> UNDERSTAND{"Understand it?"}
    UNDERSTAND -->|"No"| LADDER["'I Don't Understand' Protocol<br/>5 gradients. Still no?<br/>Post-it → Sleep → Tomorrow"]
    LADDER --> READ
    UNDERSTAND -->|"Yes"| RECALL["STEP 3: Close + Recall<br/>Stand. Walk. Say it."]
    RECALL --> WRITE["STEP 4: Write one sentence<br/>From memory"]
    WRITE --> REPEAT["STEP 5: Repeat after 10 min"]
    REPEAT --> SCHEDULE["RECALL SCHEDULE<br/>+1h → +3h → Bedtime →<br/>Next day → +3 days →<br/>+1 week → +1 month → PERMANENT"]
    SCHEDULE --> DOPAMINE["DOPAMINE REWARD<br/>Chocolate. Music. Journal."]
    DOPAMINE --> NEXT["Next chunk → Back to Step 1"]

    subgraph BAD_DAY["YELLOW/RED DAY PATH"]
        BAD_CHECK{"Brain working?"} -->|"Foggy"| VOICE_ONLY["Voice only. No writing.<br/>Review old sentences."]
        BAD_CHECK -->|"Dead"| PASSIVE["Passive only.<br/>AI reads. You follow."]
    end

    style RECALL fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style SCHEDULE fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style DOPAMINE fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff


QUICK REFERENCE: Memory System Cheat Sheet




























































I want to…Use this techniqueTargetsUnderstand a concept deeplyElaboration + “Why” chainsSemanticRemember a formula foreverSay aloud + Write 5x + Plug numbersSemantic + ProceduralRecognize problem types fastInterleaving + Compare side-by-sideImplicit (Pattern Recognition)Remember where I learned somethingPhysical anchors + Location rotationEpisodicDo a lab procedure from memoryWrite steps from memory before labProceduralRecall on command during examTrigger-action pairs + Exam simulationProspectiveNot forget after 1 day1h + 3h + Bedtime recall (non-negotiable)Short-Term → Long-TermKeep something for yearsSpaced recall: 1wk → 1mo → 3mo → 1yrLong-Term (Permanent)Study when brain is deadYellow/Red day protocol. Don’t fight it.All (damage control)Stay focused with brain fog25/5 timer + Water + Brown noise + StandingWorking Memory

THE ONLY RULE THAT MATTERS
Done is better than perfect. Known is better than unknown.
Your goal is NOT to understand everything. Your goal is NOT to remember everything.
Your goal is to know 3 things more than you knew yesterday.
If you know 3 more things, you won the day. An oral exam is 3 questions. A written exam is 3-5 problems. You don’t need 60 perfect answers. You need 3-5 solid ones.
The degree gets the job. The job doesn’t ask about your GPA after the first one.
Study. Use your voice. Move your body. Drink water. Sleep. Pass. Move on.

Method built on evidence from cognitive science: retrieval practice, spaced repetition, dual coding, elaboration, interleaving, and concrete examples (The Learning Scientists). Adapted for brains with working memory deficits, dopamine deficiency, brain fog, and severe forgetting curves.

come_studiare_prompt.title: COME_STUDIARE_PROMPT
come_studiare_prompt.content:
COME_STUDIARE: MASTER PROMPT / SPECIFICATION

Use this prompt with any AI to regenerate the COME_STUDIARE.md study method.
It captures every requirement, every memory issue, and every specification.


USER PROFILE: Cognitive & Memory Challenges
This study method is for a person with ALL of the following:
Neurotransmitter & Energy

Extremely low dopamine: brain does not naturally tag information as “important, save this”
Tired/sleepy when trying to study or be productive: mental effort triggers fatigue, not engagement

Memory Systems (All Affected)

Short-term memory problems: information disappears within seconds of reading
Long-term memory problems: information never makes it past the first 24 hours
Working memory issues: cannot hold more than 1-2 chunks in mind at once; overloads immediately
Spatial memory issues: cannot visualize or mentally manipulate objects, graphs, or layouts
Memory consolidation issues: sleep does not efficiently transfer memories from hippocampus to cortex
Recall issues: even when something IS stored, accessing it on demand is unreliable
Procedural memory weakness: difficulty translating theory into action
Episodic memory weakness: difficulty remembering context of when/where something was learned
Implicit/Pattern recognition issues: cannot see similarities across problems or identify problem types
Semantic memory weakness: difficulty connecting facts and concepts into a coherent framework
Prospective memory weakness: forgets to recall information at the right moment (exam time)

Comprehension & Processing

Cannot understand easy or hard concepts: both simple and complex ideas feel equally impenetrable
Cannot understand math, STEM subjects, or programming: technical material feels like a foreign language
Cannot understand complex or programming languages: syntax, logic, and abstraction do not compute
Problem-solving issues: cannot break down a problem into steps or see a path to the solution
Needing to read multiple times to understand/remember: one pass does nothing; even 5 passes may not work
Cannot remember things even after trying to understand: effort does not translate into retention

The “Instant Forget” Phenomenon

Reading and forgetting the next second: eyes move across words, brain retains nothing
Studying and forgetting the next second: even active study yields zero recall moments later

Focus & Attention

Issues focusing and poor concentration: attention shatters within minutes
Mind wandering: brain drifts away from the page uncontrollably
Brain fog: a thick mental haze that makes thinking feel impossible

Emotional & Stress

Chronic stress: impairs memory formation and retrieval
Anxiety: constant worry consumes cognitive resources needed for learning
Social anxiety: fear of judgment in class, labs, or presentations blocks participation
Difficulty staying calm or focused during exams: exam pressure wipes whatever memory was there


WHAT THE METHOD MUST COVER
Exam Types (ALL of them)

Oral exams (viva, presentations, discussion-based)
Written exams (essay questions, problem sets, proofs)
Theory-heavy subjects (lots of reading and concepts)
Practical/lab exams (hands-on tools, procedures)
Math and formulas
Programming and code
ANY STEM subject, no matter how hard

Memory Systems (ALL must be explicitly addressed with specific techniques)

Working Memory: micro-chunks, externalize on paper, talk aloud
Short-Term Memory: 3-pass system, immediate vocal recall
Long-Term Memory: spaced recall schedule (1h, 3h, sleep, next day, 3 days, 1 week)
Semantic Memory: elaboration, mind maps, “why” chains, compare/contrast
Episodic Memory: physical anchors, emotional hooks, location rotation, story-ify
Procedural Memory: hands-on practice, type code from scratch, execute by hand, write procedures from memory
Implicit Memory (Pattern Recognition): interleaving, compare side-by-side, categorize before solving
Prospective Memory: trigger-action pairs, exam simulation, implementation intentions (If-Then)


CORE PRINCIPLES (Non-Negotiable)


RULE ZERO: Never study in silence. Every piece of information must be read aloud, then recalled aloud without looking. Voice = dual coding (production + auditory). Whisper backup. AI voice passive mode for exhausted days.


STEP ZERO: 30-second preview before every session. Skim titles, bold words, diagrams. Say aloud what the section covers. Builds the mental shelf.


5-Step Method for every chunk of material:

Step 1: Isolate: take only 2 minutes worth, cover the rest
Step 2: Read aloud + simplify: say it like to a 10-year-old
Step 3: Close + Recall: stand up, walk, say it without looking. Struggle 10 seconds before peeking.
Step 4: Write one sentence: from memory, not copying
Step 5: Repeat after 10 minutes: check if it stuck



3-Pass System for instant-forget phenomenon:

Pass 1: Micro: one sentence aloud, close eyes, say it, move on
Pass 2: Cluster: after 5 sentences, recall all 5 in order
Pass 3: Panoramic: after whole section, recall what each title was about



Enhanced Recall Schedule (steeper forgetting curve):

Immediate → +1 hour → +3 hours → Before sleep → Next day → +3 days → +1 week → +1 month
First 4 are NON-NEGOTIABLE



“I Don’t Understand Anything” Protocol: 5-gradient ladder:

Gradient 1: Read aloud 3 times
Gradient 2: Copy by hand
Gradient 3: Find the unknown word, look it up
Gradient 4: Replace hard words with easy ones
Gradient 5: Draw it (rectangle, arrow, stickman)
If still no: post-it “TO UNDERSTAND”, move on, sleep, try tomorrow



Terrible Brain Day Protocol (Green/Yellow/Red):

Green: Full 5-step method, new material, recall schedule
Yellow: Voice only, no writing, review old sentences only
Red: Passive only, AI voice reads, follow with eyes, zero guilt



Dopamine System: artificial rewards to compensate for low dopamine:

After each Step 5: dark chocolate square
After 3 cycles: 2 min favorite song, dance
End of day: Dopamine Journal: 3 things LEARNED (not studied)



Oral Exam Specific:

3-Sentence Rule: What it is / How it works / Why it connects
Mirror technique: practice looking calm
Trigger-action pairs: “When prof says X, I say Y”
Pause strategy: silence = thoughtful, not failure
What to say if you don’t know: “I can reason through it…”
Breathing: 4-4-4-4



Practical Lab Specific:

4-step method: Read once aloud → Write steps from memory → Simulate aloud → Predict results
During exam: explain WHY after each command
If tool doesn’t work: name alternative tool, explain same logic



Body & Environment:

10 min walk before studying (BDNF boost)
8 hours sleep (memory consolidation)
No phone in bed, no screens 30 min before sleep
Water every 10 minutes (1.5L bottle on desk)
Never study right after eating (wait 30 min)
No caffeine or energy drinks
Stand or walk while studying
Brown noise background (not music, not white noise)
25 min study / 5 min break (Pomodoro)



Emergency Cramming (30 minutes: damage minimization):

Read ONLY section titles aloud
One sentence per title
Do NOT read full text
Close eyes, list titles you remember



Long-Term Memory Forever:

Expanding intervals: 1wk → 1mo → 3mo → 6mo → 1yr
Annual “brain dump”: blank sheet, write everything you remember
Missing items get one 2-min review




FORMATTING REQUIREMENTS

Language: English ONLY
No emojis anywhere: not in text, not in diagrams, not in headers
Mermaid flowcharts for all key processes (minimum 15-20 diagrams)
Tables for comparisons, schedules, cheat sheets
Bold for key terms and emphasis
Blockquotes for important takeaways
Short paragraphs (max 3-4 sentences)
Direct, concrete language: no passive voice, no academic jargon
Address the reader directly: “you”, “your brain”


REQUIRED SECTIONS (Minimum)

YOUR MEMORY ISSUES: complete list of all cognitive challenges
THE BIG PICTURE: mermaid diagram of all memory systems working together
YOUR MEMORY SYSTEMS: table: what each does, your problem, the fix
WHY NORMAL STUDYING DOESN’T WORK FOR YOU: mermaid diagram of failing methods
RULE ZERO: NEVER STUDY IN SILENCE: voice techniques, whisper, backup methods
STEP ZERO: THE 30-SECOND PREVIEW: mermaid flowchart
THE 5-STEP METHOD: mermaid flowchart + detailed steps
THE “I DON’T UNDERSTAND ANYTHING” PROTOCOL: 5-gradient ladder (mermaid)
DOPAMINE: rewards, journal, what to avoid (mermaid)
THE 3-PASS SYSTEM: mermaid flowchart
THE RECALL SCHEDULE: mermaid timeline + table
HOW TO TRAIN EACH MEMORY SYSTEM: specific techniques for all 8 systems
FOR ORAL EXAMS: prep + during (mermaid)
FOR THEORY SUBJECTS: mind maps, teach a ghost, compare/contrast, story-ify
FOR WRITTEN EXAMS: blank page method, check backwards
FOR MATH AND FORMULAS: say it, explain it, example, write 5x, throw away (mermaid)
FOR PROGRAMMING AND CODE: execute by hand, rewrite from memory, break it (mermaid)
FOR PRACTICAL STEM: before lab, during lab (mermaid)
THE “TERRIBLE BRAIN DAY” PROTOCOL: green/yellow/red decision tree (mermaid)
FOR FOCUS AND BRAIN FOG: non-negotiable rules
FOR STRESS AND ANXIETY: breathing, smiling, sleep
EMERGENCY CRAMMING: 30-minute damage minimization
LONG-TERM MEMORY: spaced repetition for lifetime retention (mermaid)
DAILY ROUTINE: morning to bedtime (mermaid)
THE COMPLETE SYSTEM: overview flowchart of everything
QUICK REFERENCE: memory system cheat sheet table
THE ONLY RULE THAT MATTERS: closing philosophy


TONE & STYLE

Direct and simple. Short sentences.
Never condescending. Never “just try harder.”
Acknowledges the brain’s limits without judgment.
“You are not broken. You are not stupid. Your brain needs a different approach.”
Uses concrete, physical metaphors (mental shelf, spare tire, save button, brain fertilizer)
Every technique has a “Why”: explains the science in 1 plain sentence
Optimistic but realistic: “Done is better than perfect. Known is better than unknown.”


SOURCE METHODOLOGY
Built on evidence-based cognitive science:

Retrieval Practice (The Learning Scientists)
Spaced Repetition (Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, expanded intervals)
Dual Coding (Paivio: verbal + visual encoding)
Elaboration (connecting new info to existing knowledge)
Interleaving (mixing problem types)
Concrete Examples (abstract → concrete)
Adapted specifically for: working memory deficits, dopamine deficiency, brain fog, severe forgetting curves, anxiety, and all 8 memory system weaknesses listed above.

blog/building-a-second-brain.title: Building a Second Brain That Actually Thinks
blog/building-a-second-brain.content:
After years of experimentation, I have arrived at a note-taking system that actually compounds. This post explains why most notes fail and what I do instead.
Most people treat notes as storage. They capture information, file it by source, and never look at it again. A year later they have a graveyard of disconnected summaries.
My notes think. They evolve. Every time I open an old note, I revise it. Every time I learn something new, I connect it. Over months and years, my notes compound: each one a brick in a structure that gets stronger, not dustier.
The core principles: one note, one idea. Organized by concept, not by source. Densely linked. Titled with a declarative claim. Written for my future self, not an audience.
Read the full system at How I Write Notes That Actually Think.
Related

how-i-write-notes
evergreen-notes
knowledge-work-compounds
morning-writing-practice

blog/morning-ritual.title: The Morning Ritual That Changed Everything
blog/morning-ritual.content:
The most productive change I made this year was not a tool or a technique. It was a time of day.
Every morning, before anything else, I spend two hours writing and revising notes. Not answering emails. Not reading Twitter. Not planning my day. Just thinking in writing: developing ideas, making connections, sharpening claims.
This is the hardest cognitive work I do, which is exactly why it comes first. By evening, my mind is depleted. Morning clarity is worth more than any productivity hack.
The ritual is simple: open the writing inbox, flip through prompts, draft an evergreen note, link it to existing notes. Repeat. Over a year, this produces a network of interconnected ideas that becomes the foundation for everything else.
Read the full practice at The Morning Practice and The Writing Inbox.
Related

morning-writing-practice
daily-log
the-writing-inbox

blog/understanding-cannot-be-downloaded.title: Why Understanding Cannot Be Downloaded
blog/understanding-cannot-be-downloaded.content:
Information is abundant. Understanding is scarce. You cannot download it, cannot copy it, cannot receive it from someone else. You have to build it yourself, piece by piece, through struggle.
This is why lectures do not work the way we think they do. Sitting in a lecture hall, listening to someone explain a concept, feels like learning. But it is not. It is recognition: the comfortable feeling of following someone else’s thinking. Real understanding only begins when you close the notebook and try to explain the idea yourself.
Writing forces this. When you sit down to write about something you think you understand, the gaps reveal themselves immediately. The fuzzy parts become obvious. The connections you thought existed turn out to be missing. Every gap you find and fill makes the understanding real.
Read more at Why Understanding Requires Struggle.
Related

understanding-requires-struggle
evergreen-notes
how-i-write-notes

come-studiare/3-pass-system.title: The 3-Pass System
come-studiare/3-pass-system.content:
The 3-Pass System
For when you read and forget the next second.
Pass 1: Micro (1 min each): Read ONE sentence aloud. Close eyes. Say it. Move on.
Pass 2: Cluster (3 min): After 5 sentences, recall ALL 5 in order. Aloud. No peeking.
Pass 3: Panoramic (5 min): After the whole section, read every title aloud. For each title, say ONE sentence about what it covered.
The “One More Time” Rule
If you finish a section and remember NOTHING: do it one more time. Not five more times. ONE. Your brain learns on the second pass, not the fifth.
Then move on. Trust that something stuck: it did.

come-studiare/5-step-method.title: The 5-Step Method
come-studiare/5-step-method.content:
The 5-Step Method
For ANY subject, no matter how hard.


BREAK: Take only 2 minutes worth. Cover the rest with paper. One chunk = your working memory can handle it.


READ ALOUD + SIMPLIFY: Say it like to a 10-year-old. If you can’t simplify it, you didn’t understand it.


CLOSE + RECALL: Stand up. Walk. Say it without looking. Struggle for 10 seconds before peeking. The pain of not remembering IS the moment you learn.


WRITE ONE SENTENCE: From memory, not copying. This becomes YOUR note. The paper is your extended brain.


REPEAT AFTER 10 MINUTES: Look at your sentence. Still there? If not, redo from Step 2.



After Step 5, write a note in this system. That note is your external memory. Over time, these notes become your personal textbook: written in YOUR words, linked by YOUR understanding.

come-studiare/index.content:

come-studiare/recall-schedule.title: The Recall Schedule
come-studiare/recall-schedule.content:
The Recall Schedule
My forgetting curve is steeper than normal. I need MORE frequent recalls in the FIRST 24 HOURS.


















































WhenActionTimeImmediateClose book, say it aloud30 sec+1 hourRead MY sentence, recall2 min+3 hoursSay concept while walking1 minBefore sleepReview ALL sentences from today aloud5 minNext dayExplain each sentence without looking5 min+3 daysQuick vocal recall3 min+1 week2-minute check2 min+1 monthFinal check1 min

The first 4 recalls are NON-NEGOTIABLE. They are the difference between remembering for 1 day vs remembering for 1 year.

courses/anno-1/algoritmi-strutture-dati.title: Algoritmi e Strutture Dati
courses/anno-1/algoritmi-strutture-dati.content:
Algoritmi e Strutture Dati
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Algoritmi di ordinamento: merge sort, quicksort, heapsort. Strutture dati: liste concatenate, pile, code, alberi binari di ricerca, heap, tabelle hash, grafi. Analisi di complessità computazionale (notazione O grande). Algoritmi su grafi: BFS, DFS, Dijkstra. Programmazione dinamica e greedy.
Competenze acquisite
Analisi della complessità temporale e spaziale degli algoritmi, scelta della struttura dati appropriata per un problema dato, implementazione efficiente di algoritmi classici in C o Python.

courses/anno-1/architettura-elaboratori.title: Elementi di Architettura degli Elaboratori e Sistemi Operativi
courses/anno-1/architettura-elaboratori.content:
Elementi di Architettura degli Elaboratori e Sistemi Operativi
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Architettura di von Neumann: CPU, memoria, bus, I/O. Rappresentazione dell’informazione: numeri binari, esadecimali, complemento a due, virgola mobile (IEEE 754). Algebra booleana e porte logiche. Circuiti combinatori e sequenziali. Il set di istruzioni: RISC vs CISC, formati, modi di indirizzamento. Pipeline e hazard. Gerarchia di memoria: cache, memoria virtuale, TLB. Sistemi operativi: processi, scheduling, file system.
Competenze acquisite
Comprensione del funzionamento interno di un calcolatore, analisi delle prestazioni di un processore, progettazione di semplici circuiti digitali, comprensione della gestione della memoria e dei processi.

courses/anno-1/basi-di-dati.title: Basi di Dati
courses/anno-1/basi-di-dati.content:
Basi di Dati
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Modello relazionale: tabelle, chiavi, vincoli di integrità. Algebra relazionale e calcolo relazionale. Linguaggio SQL: DDL, DML, DCL. Progettazione concettuale con il modello Entità-Relazione. Normalizzazione: 1FN, 2FN, 3FN, BCNF. Transazioni e controllo di concorrenza. Cenni a database NoSQL.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione di database relazionali normalizzati, scrittura di query SQL complesse, gestione delle transazioni, modellazione concettuale di domini applicativi.

courses/anno-1/index.title: Anno 1: Corsi
courses/anno-1/index.content:
Anno 1: Programmazione e Gestione dei Sistemi Informatici
A.A. 2024-2025 | Università degli Studi di Perugia | Classe L-P03

courses/anno-1/lab-architettura.title: Lab. Architettura degli Elaboratori
courses/anno-1/lab-architettura.content:
Lab. Architettura degli Elaboratori
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Elementi di Architettura degli Elaboratori e Sistemi Operativi
Contenuti
Laboratorio di architettura dei calcolatori. Simulazione di circuiti logici con Logisim. Assembly x86: registri, istruzioni, stack, chiamate di sistema. Programmazione di microcontrollori. Interfacciamento hardware-software.
Competenze acquisite
Programmazione in linguaggio assembly, comprensione del funzionamento della CPU a basso livello, progettazione di circuiti digitali, programmazione embedded base.

courses/anno-1/lab-basi-di-dati.title: Lab. Basi di Dati
courses/anno-1/lab-basi-di-dati.content:
Lab. Basi di Dati
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Basi di Dati
Contenuti
SQL pratico: SELECT complesse, JOIN, subquery, aggregazioni GROUP BY, HAVING. Progettazione ER: entità, relazioni, cardinalità. Normalizzazione pratica: dalla 1FN alla 3FN. Implementazione in PostgreSQL. Creazione di viste, indici, stored procedure.
Competenze acquisite
Scrittura di query SQL complesse su database reali, progettazione di schemi normalizzati, ottimizzazione delle interrogazioni, gestione di database relazionali.

courses/anno-1/lab-hci.title: Lab. HCI: Human Computer Interaction
courses/anno-1/lab-hci.content:
Lab. HCI: Human Computer Interaction
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Principi di usabilità: Nielsen’s heuristics, affordance, feedback. User research: interviste, personas, scenari d’uso. Prototipazione: wireframe, mockup, prototipi interattivi con Figma. Valutazione: test di usabilità, think-aloud protocol, heuristic evaluation. Accessibilità: WCAG, screen reader, color contrast.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione centrata sull’utente, creazione di prototipi interattivi, conduzione di test di usabilità, valutazione dell’accessibilità di interfacce.

courses/anno-1/lab-open-source.title: Lab. Open Source
courses/anno-1/lab-open-source.content:
Lab. Open Source
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Git e GitHub: clone, commit, push, pull, branch, merge, pull request, risoluzione conflitti. Linux shell: navigazione, permessi, pipe, scripting bash, variabili d’ambiente. Markdown e documentazione tecnica. Licenze open source: GPL, MIT, Apache, Creative Commons. Contribuzione a progetti open source: fork, issue, pull request.
Competenze acquisite
Gestione professionale del version control, amministrazione base Linux, scrittura di documentazione in Markdown, contribuzione a repository pubbliche su GitHub.

courses/anno-1/lab-programmazione-1.title: Lab. Programmazione I
courses/anno-1/lab-programmazione-1.content:
Lab. Programmazione I
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Programmazione I
Contenuti
Esercitazioni C: tipi, operatori, controllo di flusso. Funzioni e parametri. Array e stringhe. Puntatori e gestione dinamica della memoria. Struct e tipi definiti dall’utente. File I/O. Debugging con GDB e Valgrind. Makefile per compilazione automatica.
Competenze acquisite
Scrittura di programmi C corretti ed efficienti, debugging sistematico, gestione manuale della memoria senza leak, automazione della compilazione.

courses/anno-1/lab-reti.title: Lab. Reti
courses/anno-1/lab-reti.content:
Lab. Reti
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Reti di Computer
Contenuti
Configurazione di reti LAN con Packet Tracer. Subnetting IPv4 e IPv6. Analisi del traffico con Wireshark: cattura e filtraggio pacchetti. Socket programming: client-server TCP e UDP in C. Protocolli applicativi: HTTP, DNS, DHCP in pratica.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione e configurazione di reti, analisi di protocolli a livello di pacchetto, programmazione di applicazioni di rete, troubleshooting di connettività.

courses/anno-1/lab-vr-ar-1.title: Lab. Realtà Virtuale e Aumentata I
courses/anno-1/lab-vr-ar-1.content:
Lab. Realtà Virtuale e Aumentata I
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Unity 3D: interfaccia, GameObject, componenti, prefab, scene. Scripting in C# per Unity: MonoBehaviour, Update, FixedUpdate. Fisica e collisioni: Rigidbody, Collider, triggers. Raycasting e interazione con oggetti 3D. Sviluppo per visori VR: Oculus Quest, interazione con controller, locomotion. Realtà aumentata: AR Foundation, plane detection, image tracking.
Competenze acquisite
Sviluppo di esperienze VR/AR in Unity, programmazione C# per ambienti 3D, design di interfacce utente spaziali, ottimizzazione per dispositivi VR.

courses/anno-1/lingua-inglese.title: Lingua Inglese B2
courses/anno-1/lingua-inglese.content:
Lingua Inglese: Livello B2
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU | Livello B1/B2
Contenuti
Reading comprehension di testi tecnico-scientifici in informatica. Academic writing: struttura di report, essay, abstract. Listening: conferenze e presentazioni tecniche, TED talks. Speaking: presentazioni orali su argomenti IT, discussioni di gruppo. Vocabolario tecnico: terminology specifica di programmazione, reti, AI, cybersecurity.
Competenze acquisite
Comprensione e produzione di testi tecnici in inglese, presentazioni orali professionali, partecipazione a discussioni tecniche in lingua inglese, scrittura di documentazione tecnica.

courses/anno-1/matematica-informatica.title: Matematica per l'Informatica
courses/anno-1/matematica-informatica.content:
Matematica per l’Informatica
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | MAT/03 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Logica proposizionale e predicativa: connettivi, quantificatori, tavole di verità. Teoria degli insiemi: operazioni, relazioni, funzioni. Induzione matematica e definizioni ricorsive. Combinatoria: permutazioni, combinazioni, principio dei cassetti. Teoria dei grafi: rappresentazioni, cammini, alberi, colorazione. Probabilità discreta.
Competenze acquisite
Ragionamento logico-formale, dimostrazioni matematiche, modellazione di problemi con grafi, calcolo combinatorio, fondamenti probabilistici per l’informatica.

courses/anno-1/programmazione-1.title: Programmazione I
courses/anno-1/programmazione-1.content:
Programmazione I
Anno 1: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Fondamenti di programmazione in linguaggio C. Tipi di dato, operatori, espressioni. Strutture di controllo: if, switch, while, for. Funzioni e passaggio di parametri. Array, stringhe, struct. Puntatori e aritmetica dei puntatori. Allocazione dinamica: malloc, calloc, free. File I/O. Ricorsione. Cenni di complessità computazionale.
Competenze acquisite
Padronanza della sintassi e semantica del linguaggio C, risoluzione di problemi attraverso la programmazione strutturata, gestione manuale della memoria, debugging di programmi.

courses/anno-1/reti-computer.title: Reti di Computer ed Internet
courses/anno-1/reti-computer.content:
Reti di Computer ed Internet
Anno 1: Secondo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Architettura di rete a strati: modello OSI e TCP/IP. Livello fisico: mezzi trasmissivi, codifica dei segnali. Livello data link: framing, controllo degli errori, protocolli ARQ. Livello rete: indirizzamento IPv4/IPv6, routing (RIP, OSPF, BGP). Livello trasporto: TCP e UDP, controllo di congestione. Livello applicazione: HTTP, DNS, SMTP.
Competenze acquisite
Comprensione del funzionamento di Internet a tutti i livelli, calcolo di sottoreti e piani di indirizzamento, analisi dei protocolli di rete, diagnosi di problemi di connettività.

courses/anno-2/cloud-computing.title: Cloud Computing
courses/anno-2/cloud-computing.content:
Cloud Computing
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU | INGLESE
Contenuti
Modelli di servizio cloud: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS. Virtualizzazione: hypervisor, container. Architetture cloud-native: microservizi, serverless, event-driven. Scalabilità orizzontale e verticale, load balancing, auto-scaling. Storage cloud: object storage, block storage, database managed. Reti cloud: VPC, CDN, DNS. Sicurezza nel cloud: shared responsibility model, IAM, encryption. Economia del cloud: modelli di costo, ottimizzazione.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione di architetture cloud scalabili, selezione dei servizi cloud appropriati, stima e ottimizzazione dei costi, implementazione di strategie di disaster recovery.

courses/anno-2/cybersecurity.title: Cybersecurity
courses/anno-2/cybersecurity.content:
Cybersecurity
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU | INGLESE
Contenuti
Principi fondamentali della sicurezza informatica: confidenzialità, integrità, disponibilità. Crittografia simmetrica e asimmetrica: AES, RSA, ECC. Funzioni di hash e firma digitale. Infrastruttura a chiave pubblica (PKI) e certificati digitali. Sicurezza delle reti: firewall, IDS/IPS, VPN, TLS. Sicurezza delle applicazioni web: OWASP Top 10. Autenticazione e controllo degli accessi. Sicurezza dei sistemi operativi.
Competenze acquisite
Analisi delle minacce alla sicurezza, progettazione di sistemi crittografici, valutazione della sicurezza di applicazioni web, configurazione di difese di rete.

courses/anno-2/index.title: Anno 2: Corsi
courses/anno-2/index.content:
Anno 2: Programmazione e Gestione dei Sistemi Informatici
A.A. 2025-2026 | Università degli Studi di Perugia | Classe L-P03
Primo Semestre

courses/anno-2/ingegneria-del-software.title: Ingegneria del Software
courses/anno-2/ingegneria-del-software.content:
Ingegneria del Software
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Ciclo di vita del software: waterfall, agile, DevOps. Analisi e specifica dei requisiti: casi d’uso, user stories. Progettazione architetturale: pattern MVC, layered, microservizi. UML: diagrammi di classe, sequenza, attività. Qualità del software: metriche, code review, refactoring. Testing: unit, integration, system, acceptance. Manutenzione ed evoluzione del software. Gestione della configurazione e versioning.
Competenze acquisite
Analisi dei requisiti e progettazione di sistemi software complessi, applicazione di metodologie di sviluppo, valutazione della qualità del codice, pianificazione di test.

courses/anno-2/lab-cloud.title: Lab. Cloud Computing
courses/anno-2/lab-cloud.content:
Lab. Cloud Computing
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Cloud Computing
Contenuti
Laboratorio pratico di cloud computing. Utilizzo di AWS (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS). Containerizzazione con Docker: Dockerfile, docker-compose, volumi, reti. Orchestrazione con Kubernetes base. CI/CD con GitHub Actions. Infrastructure as Code con Terraform.
Competenze acquisite
Deployment di applicazioni su AWS, containerizzazione di servizi, automazione delle pipeline di build e deploy, gestione di infrastrutture cloud con approccio dichiarativo.

courses/anno-2/lab-cybersecurity.title: Lab. Cybersecurity
courses/anno-2/lab-cybersecurity.content:
Lab. Cybersecurity
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Cybersecurity
Contenuti
Laboratorio pratico di sicurezza informatica. Penetration testing con Kali Linux. Analisi di vulnerabilità con Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite. Crittografia applicata: TLS, hashing, firma digitale. Sicurezza web: XSS, SQL injection, CSRF. Hardening di sistemi Linux.
Competenze acquisite
Conduzione di penetration test, identificazione e sfruttamento di vulnerabilità comuni, configurazione di firewall e IDS, applicazione delle best practice di sicurezza.

courses/anno-2/lab-gestione-emergenza.title: Lab. Gestione dell'Emergenza
courses/anno-2/lab-gestione-emergenza.content:
Lab. Gestione dell’Emergenza
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Laboratorio sulla gestione delle emergenze con tecnologie ICT. Sistemi di early warning e allertamento. Simulazioni di scenari di emergenza con strumenti digitali. Utilizzo di GIS per la mappatura dei rischi. Protocolli di comunicazione in emergenza. Analisi post-emergenza e lesson learned. Tecnologie per il coordinamento dei soccorsi.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione di sistemi ICT per la gestione delle emergenze, utilizzo di strumenti GIS, pianificazione della risposta a scenari di crisi, coordinamento tecnico in situazioni di emergenza simulate.

courses/anno-2/lab-ingegneria-software.title: Lab. Ingegneria del Software
courses/anno-2/lab-ingegneria-software.content:
Lab. Ingegneria del Software
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Collegato a: Ingegneria del Software
Contenuti
Laboratorio di ingegneria del software. Lavoro in team con metodologia Agile/Scrum. Gestione del progetto con GitHub Projects e issue tracking. Documentazione di requisiti e architettura software. Implementazione di design pattern (MVC, Observer, Factory). Testing: unit test, integration test, end-to-end test.
Competenze acquisite
Lavoro in team di sviluppo Agile, scrittura di specifiche tecniche, applicazione di design pattern in progetti reali, testing automatizzato su più livelli.

courses/anno-2/lab-programmazione-web-1.title: Lab. Programmazione Web I
courses/anno-2/lab-programmazione-web-1.content:
Lab. Programmazione Web I
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
HTML5 semantico e form. CSS3: Flexbox, Grid, animazioni, media queries. JavaScript ES6+: DOM manipulation, eventi, fetch API, async/await. LocalStorage e sessionStorage. Introduzione a React: componenti, state, props, hooks base. Webpack e Vite come bundler.
Competenze acquisite
Costruzione di interfacce responsive, programmazione JavaScript moderna, comunicazione client-server asincrona, basi di sviluppo con React.

courses/anno-2/lab-programmazione-web-2.title: Lab. Programmazione Web II
courses/anno-2/lab-programmazione-web-2.content:
Lab. Programmazione Web II
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Backend con Node.js ed Express: routing, middleware, autenticazione JWT, API RESTful. Database con MongoDB e Mongoose: schema, CRUD, aggregazioni. Full-stack: integrazione frontend React con backend Express. Testing: unit test con Jest, integration test con Supertest. Deploy: Vercel, Railway, variabili d’ambiente.
Competenze acquisite
Sviluppo full-stack JavaScript, progettazione API RESTful, integrazione database, testing automatizzato, deployment di applicazioni web.

courses/anno-2/lab-sistemi-iot.title: Lab. Sistemi IoT
courses/anno-2/lab-sistemi-iot.content:
Lab. Sistemi IoT
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Laboratorio di Internet of Things. Programmazione di ESP32 e Arduino con C/C++. Sensori e attuatori: temperatura, umidità, movimento, display. Protocolli IoT: MQTT, CoAP, BLE. Comunicazione con il cloud: invio dati a database remoti. Dashboard di monitoraggio con Node-RED.
Competenze acquisite
Programmazione di microcontrollori, integrazione di sensori e attuatori, progettazione di architetture IoT end-to-end, visualizzazione di dati da sensori in tempo reale.

courses/anno-2/lab-vr-ar-2.title: Lab. Realtà Virtuale e Aumentata II
courses/anno-2/lab-vr-ar-2.content:
Lab. Realtà Virtuale e Aumentata II
Anno 2: Secondo Semestre | 3 CFU
Contenuti
Sviluppo avanzato di applicazioni VR/AR. Realtà aumentata con ARCore e ARKit. Sviluppo per visori standalone (Meta Quest). Ottimizzazione delle performance: LOD, occlusion culling, texture atlasing. Multiplayer in VR: sincronizzazione, networked physics. Interaction design avanzato.
Competenze acquisite
Sviluppo di applicazioni AR mobile, ottimizzazione avanzata per VR, implementazione di esperienze multiplayer in realtà virtuale, progettazione di interfacce utente spaziali.

courses/anno-2/machine-learning.title: Machine Learning
courses/anno-2/machine-learning.content:
Machine Learning
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU | INGLESE
Contenuti
Supervised learning: regressione lineare e logistica, SVM, alberi di decisione, random forest, k-NN. Unsupervised learning: k-means, PCA, clustering gerarchico. Neural networks: perceptron, backpropagation, deep learning (cenni). Model evaluation: cross-validation, bias-variance tradeoff, precision/recall, ROC. Regularization: L1, L2, dropout. Introduction to TensorFlow and scikit-learn.
Competenze acquisite
Implementazione di modelli ML in Python, selezione e tuning degli iperparametri, valutazione delle performance, comprensione teorica degli algoritmi principali.

courses/anno-2/programmazione-2.title: Programmazione II
courses/anno-2/programmazione-2.content:
Programmazione II
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti
Programmazione orientata agli oggetti in Java. Classi, oggetti, incapsulamento, ereditarietà, polimorfismo. Interfacce e classi astratte. Eccezioni e gestione degli errori. Collezioni: List, Set, Map, Queue. Stream e lambda expression. Programmazione concorrente con i thread. Design pattern fondamentali.
Competenze acquisite
Progettazione orientata agli oggetti, implementazione di gerarchie di classi ben strutturate, programmazione concorrente, utilizzo delle API standard di Java.

courses/anno-2/sistemi-elettronici-senso.title: Sistemi Elettronici e Sensori
courses/anno-2/sistemi-elettronici-senso.content:
Sistemi Elettronici e Sensori per l’Informatica
Anno 2: Primo Semestre | ING-INF/01 | 6 CFU
Contenuti**
Fondamenti di elettronica analogica e digitale. Sensori: temperatura, pressione, accelerometri, giroscopi, sensori ottici. Condizionamento del segnale: amplificazione, filtraggio, conversione A/D e D/A. Microcontrollori e sistemi embedded. Protocolli di comunicazione: I2C, SPI, UART. Sistemi di acquisizione dati. Elaborazione del segnale digitale (cenni).
Competenze acquisite
Scelta e integrazione di sensori per applicazioni specifiche, progettazione di circuiti di condizionamento, programmazione di sistemi di acquisizione dati, elaborazione base dei segnali.

math/algebra/index.title: Algebra: Complete Reference
math/algebra/index.content:
Algebra
Foundation for all STEM coursework. Covers the essential algebraic techniques required for calculus, linear algebra, and machine learning.
Topics
1. Preliminaries

Exponent Properties, Rational Exponents, Negative Exponents
Radicals, Polynomials, Factoring
Rational Expressions, Complex Numbers

2. Solving Equations and Inequalities

Linear Equations, Quadratic Equations
Completing the Square, Quadratic Formula
Applications, Reducible to Quadratic Form
Equations with Radicals, Linear Inequalities
Polynomial & Rational Inequalities
Absolute Value Equations & Inequalities

3. Graphing and Functions

Graphing Lines, Circles, Piecewise Functions
Function Definition, Notation, Composition
Inverse Functions

4. Common Graphs

Parabolas, Ellipses, Hyperbolas
Absolute Value, Square Root, Constant Function
Rational Functions, Shifts, Reflections, Symmetry

5. Polynomial Functions

Dividing Polynomials, Zeroes/Roots
Finding Zeroes, Graphing Polynomials
Partial Fractions

6. Exponential and Logarithm Functions

Exponential Functions, Logarithm Functions
Solving Exponential/Logarithm Equations
Applications

7. Systems of Equations

Substitution Method, Elimination Method
Augmented Matrix, Nonlinear Systems

math/calculus-1/index.title: Calculus I: Complete Reference
math/calculus-1/index.content:
Calculus I
Single-variable calculus: limits, derivatives, integrals, and their applications. Essential for machine learning optimization, gradient descent, and probability theory.
Topics
1. Review

Trig Review: Trig functions, Exponential/Logarithm functions

2. Limits

Concepts, Definition, Computing Limits
One-Sided Limits, Continuity
Limits Involving Infinity, L’Hospital’s Rule

3. Derivatives

Definition, Interpretations, Derivative Formulas
Power Rule, Product Rule, Quotient Rule, Chain Rule
Higher Order Derivatives, Implicit Differentiation
Logarithmic Differentiation
Derivatives of Trig, Exponential, Logarithm, Inverse Trig, Hyperbolic Trig Functions

4. Applications of Derivatives

Related Rates, Critical Points
Minimum/Maximum Values, Increasing/Decreasing Functions
Inflection Points, Concavity, Optimization

5. Integrals

Definition, Indefinite Integrals, Definite Integrals
Substitution Rule, Evaluating Definite Integrals
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

6. Applications of Integrals

Average Function Value, Area Between Curves
Solids of Revolution, Work

math/calculus-2/index.title: Calculus II: Complete Reference
math/calculus-2/index.content:
Calculus II
Advanced integration techniques, sequences and series, parametric equations, and vectors. Critical for understanding probability distributions, signal processing, and numerical methods in ML.
Topics
1. Integration Techniques

Integration by Parts
Integrals Involving Trig Functions
Trig Substitutions, Partial Fractions
Integrals Involving Roots and Quadratics
Integration Strategy, Improper Integrals
Comparison Test, Approximating Definite Integrals

2. Applications of Integrals

Arc Length, Surface Area
Center of Mass/Centroid
Hydrostatic Pressure and Force, Probability

3. Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates

Parametric Equations & Curves
Calculus with Parametrics (Tangents, Areas, Arc Length, Surface Area)
Polar Coordinates, Calculus with Polar Coordinates

4. Sequences and Series

Sequences, Series, Convergence/Divergence
Integral Test, Comparison Test, Limit Comparison Test

notes/against-folders.title: Let Structure Emerge: Against Folders and Categories
notes/against-folders.content:
Do not organize your notes into folders. Do not create categories. Let structure emerge from the bottom up, through links, over time.
The Problem with Folders
A folder hierarchy forces you to answer a question prematurely: “Where does this belong?” You must choose exactly one place for each note, at the moment of creation, before you fully understand what the note is about or how it connects to everything else.
This is destructive for three reasons.
First, most ideas belong in multiple places. A note about derivatives might belong under Mathematics, under Physics (as velocity), under Economics (as marginal cost), and under Machine Learning (as gradient descent). A folder forces you to pick one. The other connections are lost: or at least hidden, accessible only through memory.
Second, folder hierarchies fossilize. Once you have a structure, new notes get filed into existing categories. Novel connections that do not fit the hierarchy become invisible. The structure stops evolving precisely when it should be growing most rapidly.
Third, folders make you think about organization instead of thinking about ideas. “Where should I file this?” is a clerical question. “What does this connect to?” is a thinking question. The first distracts from the second.
Links Instead of Categories
The alternative is a flat space of notes connected by explicit links. Each note declares its relationships directly: “this idea depends on that one,” “this is a counterexample to that claim,” “this generalizes that concept.”
These links are the organization. They form a web, not a tree. The web can have cycles. A note can link to ten other notes, and ten notes can link back. Multiple paths can lead to the same idea from different directions.
This is how your brain actually works: through association, not hierarchy. When you think of one concept, related concepts activate. The connections are not organized in a tidy tree. They are a network.
Letting Categories Emerge
After writing notes for months or years, clusters naturally form. You notice that twenty notes all orbit around a central concept. Now you can name that cluster. Now you can write a hub note that links to all of them and explains how they relate.
The difference is that the category emerged from the content, rather than being imposed before the content existed. It describes what is actually there, rather than constraining what can be there.
This is the principle of associative ontologies: let the structure of your knowledge be discovered, not designed. The web of links between atomic notes IS the taxonomy. It is richer, more flexible, and more truthful than any folder hierarchy could be.
What This Looks Like in Practice
I never ask “where should I put this note?” The answer is always the same: in the flat notes directory, with a declarative title and links to whatever it connects to.
When I need to find something, I do not browse folders. I search. I follow links. I use the graph view to see clusters. The navigation is associative: I start from one idea and follow the trails to others.
This feels unfamiliar at first. We are trained to think in hierarchies: folders, outlines, tree structures. But the discomfort is productive. It forces you to think about how ideas actually relate, rather than where they can be conveniently filed.
Related

evergreen-notes
how-i-write-notes
note-titles-as-apis

notes/better-thinking-not-notes.title: The Goal Is Better Thinking, Not Better Notes
notes/better-thinking-not-notes.content:
The Goal Is Better Thinking, Not Better Notes
People write endlessly about how to take better notes. What app to use. How to organize folders. Whether to use tags or links. What template produces the best results. The questions are always about the container, never about the contents.
These questions have no satisfying answers because they ask the wrong thing. The goal is not to take better notes. The goal is to think more effectively.
The Visible Component of an Invisible Practice
Note-taking is visible. You can see someone writing in a notebook and think: if I get that notebook and organize it the same way, I will be insightful too. The notebook is tangible. Buying it feels like doing something. Setting up a note-taking system feels productive even when you have not written a single useful thought.
Thinking is invisible. You cannot see someone work through a hard problem in their head. You cannot photograph the moment an insight crystallizes. There is no product to buy that will make you a better thinker.
So people focus on notes. It is easier. It feels like progress. And it mostly leads nowhere.
The Questions That Actually Matter
Instead of asking “how should I organize my notes?”, ask:

What practices help me develop insights reliably over time?
How do I shepherd my attention toward what matters?
When I encounter a new idea, how do I integrate it with what I already know?
How do I make sure my thinking today builds on my thinking yesterday?

These are harder questions. They do not have simple answers that fit in a productivity blog post. But they point toward the real work: building a system that helps you think, not a system that helps you file.
The Technology Trap
The same confusion appears in conversations about tools. People ask “what is the best note-taking app?” as if the app does the thinking for them. They ask “can this tool generate connections automatically?” as if machine learning can substitute for the hard work of understanding how ideas relate.
What matters is not “computer-supported note-taking” but “computer-supported thinking.” The tool should serve the thinking, not the other way around.
A simple text file with clear, linked, well-written notes beats the most sophisticated app filled with disorganized, unprocessed material. The medium is secondary. The thinking is primary.
Why This Is Hard to See
Note-taking provides weak feedback. You can take terrible notes for years and never notice, because you never go back and try to use them. You do not see that your notes are disorganized because you never need to find anything. You do not realize your notes are shallow because you never try to build an argument from them.
Real thinking provides strong feedback. When you try to write a coherent essay from your notes, you immediately discover what you do not understand. When you try to explain a concept to someone else, the gaps become obvious. When you revisit a note after six months and find it incomprehensible, you learn something about how to write.
Focus on the thinking. The note quality will follow.
Related

how-i-write-notes
knowledge-work-accretes
understanding-requires-struggle
evergreen-notes

notes/collectors-fallacy.title: The Collector's Fallacy: Why Saving Is Not Learning
notes/collectors-fallacy.content:
The Collector’s Fallacy: Why Saving Is Not Learning
You have a folder of PDFs you have never read. A bookmark library with hundreds of articles you “want to get to.” A reading list that grows faster than you can read. Highlighted passages in two dozen books, none of which you could summarize from memory.
This feels productive. It is not. It is the Collector’s Fallacy: the false belief that having access to information is the same as knowing it.
Why Collecting Feels So Good
Collecting is easy. Clicking “save” takes zero cognitive effort. The act of saving quells the anxiety of losing something potentially valuable. Each bookmark provides a small hit of satisfaction: I might need this someday, and now I will be able to find it.
But the satisfying feeling is a lie. Having a text at hand does nothing to increase your knowledge. The information lives on your hard drive, not in your brain. When you need it, you will not remember it exists. Or you will remember it exists but not remember what it said. Or you will find it but not understand it because you never processed it.
This is why so many people feel overwhelmed by their “to read” piles. The pile is not a sign of diligence. It is a sign of avoidance. Collecting is a substitute for thinking.
The Real Cost
Collecting has three hidden costs:
It buries what matters. When you save everything, you save nothing. The genuinely important material gets buried under tons of secondary matter you saved just in case. Months later, you cannot find what you need because there is too much to search through.
It creates false confidence. You feel knowledgeable because you have read ten articles about a topic. But reading is not learning. You recognized the ideas as they flowed past. You did not encode them, connect them, or retrieve them. Ask yourself a question about the topic right now. Can you answer it clearly? If not, you collected, not learned.
It wastes time on the wrong things. The impulse to save everything means you spend time on material that is not worth deep engagement. Most texts are not worth detailed notes. But if you save everything, you treat everything as equally important, which means you treat nothing as important.
What to Do Instead
The antidote is simple but hard: process, do not hoard. When you find something worth keeping, do one of three things immediately:

Write a brief note in your own words about what matters and why
Capture a few key details in a spaced repetition system
Decide it is not worth saving and let it go

The goal is not to have access to more information. The goal is to have more understanding. Understanding comes from engaging with ideas, not from filing them.
Short cycles work better than long ones. Process what you collect within days, not months. If a saved article sits unprocessed for a week, admit you are never going to process it and delete it. The anxiety of losing it is worse than the actual loss.
The Test
Here is the test of whether you are collecting or learning: close everything. Wait a day. Write down what you remember from what you collected, in your own words, without looking.
If you cannot do this, you collected. If you can, you learned. The size of your archive means nothing. The clarity of your recall means everything.
Related

write-to-internalize
writing-from-reading
processing-reading
understanding-requires-struggle
knowledge-work-accretes

A unified synthesis of every evidence-based learning framework. Built from 30+ sources across cognitive psychology, spaced repetition research, and real-world student methods.

    DUN & LS & SM & GOAT --> METHODS

    ZK & EV & ORG --> QTZ

The 6 Core Strategies (Learning Scientists)
These are the ONLY strategies with strong scientific evidence. Everything else builds on these.








































StrategyWhat It IsOur ImplementationSpaced PracticeSpread study over time. Never cram.[[come-studiare/recall-scheduleRetrieval PracticeBring info to mind from memory. No book.[[come-studiare/5-step-methodElaborationAsk why. Connect new to old.”Why chains”, teach a ghost, notes are linked togetherInterleavingMix problem types. Don’t block.[[come-studiare/indexConcrete ExamplesAbstract → concrete. Real world.”My example” in every note templateDual CodingWords + visuals together.Voices + writing + Quartz graph

How to Combine Them (Learning Scientists)

Spacing is the container: it’s about WHEN, not HOW. Every other strategy must be spaced.
Retrieval practice integrates with ALL: elaborate from memory, sketch from memory, create examples from memory.
Dual coding amplifies everything: add visuals to elaboration, examples, and retrieval.
Concrete examples + elaboration: trade examples with peers, explain how they fit.


Memory Systems and How to Train Each

GOAT ME: Study Based on How Memory Works (u/Salticido)
Memory = Attention → Encoding → Retrieval. Optimize the first two.

Songs, stories, doodles, personal connections.

SuperMemo 20 Rules (Wozniak, 1999)
The definitive guide to formulating knowledge for spaced repetition.

























































































#Rule1Do not learn if you do not understand2Learn before you memorize: build overall picture first3Build upon the basics4Stick to minimum information principle5Cloze deletion is easy and effective6Use imagery7Use mnemonic techniques8Graphic deletion = cloze deletion for images9Avoid sets: convert to enumerations10Avoid enumerations: use overlapping cloze11Combat interference: similar items confuse12Optimize wording: fewer words = faster13Refer to other memories14Personalize and provide examples15Rely on emotional states16Context cues simplify wording17Redundancy ≠ violation of minimum information18Provide sources19Provide date stamping20Prioritize: you can’t learn everything

Zettelkasten / Evergreen Notes Principles

Our Note-Taking Workflow

The Note Pipeline
flowchart LR
    LECTURE["Lecture<br/>Listen, don't write"] --> RECALL["Same Evening<br/>Write FROM MEMORY"]
    RECALL --> NOTE["Create Note<br/>One concept per file"]
    NOTE --> LINK["Add wikilinks<br/>Connect to existing notes"]

Anki Card States
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: Card created
    New --> Learning: First review
    Learning --> Learning: Again (step 1)
    Learning --> Review: Good (graduates)
    Learning --> Review: Easy (graduates)
    Review --> Review: Good/Easy
    Review --> Relearn: Again (forgot)
    Relearn --> Relearn: Again (step 1)
    Relearn --> Review: Good (graduates)
    Review --> [*]: Suspended

The Complete Note-Taking State Machine
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Inbox: Capture fleeting thought
    Inbox --> Literature: Process reading notes
    Literature --> Zettel: Atomize into concepts
    Zettel --> Structure: Patterns emerge
    Structure --> Output: Write/publish
    Output --> Review: Spaced recall
    Review --> Zettel: Update & refine
    Zettel --> [*]: Permanent knowledge

Every note should follow this structure:
---
title: Concept Name
course: Course Name
date: 2026-04-01
---
 
# Concept Name
 
**What it is:** (One sentence)
 
**How it works:** (Two sentences: like to a 10-year-old)
 
**Why it connects:** (One sentence: link to other notes)
 
**My example:** (A concrete, personal, stupid example)

What NEVER Works

Silent reading
Highlighting/underlining
Copying word-for-word
Cramming all night
Studying 3+ hours nonstop
Rereading without retrieval
Shared Anki decks without understanding
Learning styles (visual/auditory/kinesthetic: debunked)
Passive summarization


What ALWAYS Works

Retrieval practice (close book, recall)
Spaced repetition (distributed over time)
Elaboration (why chains, teaching)
Dual coding (voice + visuals + writing)
Concrete examples (personal, real-world)
Interleaving (mix problem types)
Sleep (memory consolidation)
Exercise (BDNF = brain fertilizer)
Water (dehydration = brain fog)


This unified framework synthesizes: Learning Scientists (6 strategies), Dunlosky et al. (2013), SuperMemo 20 Rules (Wozniak), GOAT ME method (u/Salticido), Make It Stick (Brown), Zettelkasten/Evergreen Notes (Luhmann/Matuschak), our note-taking workflow, Anki FSRS, and the COME STUDIARE method.
