notes/daily-log.title: The Daily Log: A Place for Everything
notes/daily-log.content:
Every day, I start a note titled with the date. It captures everything: reflections, scratch work, half-formed thoughts, things I noticed, things I am confused about, things I want to remember.
This is the lowest-fidelity layer of my system. It is deliberately unstructured. There is no pressure to make it coherent. It is an intentional dumping ground: a release valve so there is always a place to put that thing.
Why the Daily Log Exists
The daily log solves a simple problem: ideas appear at random times, and if you do not capture them immediately, they disappear. But you cannot stop everything to develop each idea into a proper note. The daily log is the capture zone. Zero friction. A single line is enough.
The Ladder
The daily log is the bottom of a ladder. Most entries live and die within the day: they served their purpose by getting a thought out of my head. But some entries have legs. A scratch thought crystallizes over days or weeks. At that point, I extract it from the daily log and move it into my writing inbox. From there, it may become an evergreen note.
The ladder: ephemeral scratching → inbox prompt → atomic claim → fully developed idea.
The Natural Pressure
The daily log is comfortable: easy to dump things into. But it also feels impermanent. This creates a useful pressure. Every few days, I scan recent logs. The entries that still seem interesting get promoted. The rest get archived. The system cleans itself through design, not discipline.
As Drafting Space
Sometimes I use the daily log as a drafting space. I write a rough version of an idea directly in the log: messy, unstructured, stream-of-consciousness. If it turns out well, I extract it into an evergreen note. The log absorbs the mess. The note captures the result.
Related

morning-writing-practice
the-writing-inbox

notes/do-your-own-thinking.title: Do Your Own Thinking: Why You Must Write, Not Just Read
notes/do-your-own-thinking.content:
Do Your Own Thinking: Why You Must Write, Not Just Read
Schopenhauer wrote that reading lets someone else do your thinking for you. When you read, you repeat the author’s mental process instead of performing your own. The more you read without thinking, the more you lose the ability to think independently. Schopenhauer put it bluntly: scholars who read constantly and think rarely have “read themselves stupid.”
This is not an argument against reading. It is an argument against reading as a substitute for thinking.
The Trap of Other People’s Thoughts
When you read passively, the author’s framework becomes your framework. Their categories become your categories. Their conclusions become your conclusions. You are not learning. You are absorbing. There is a difference.
Absorbing feels like learning because the ideas are clear in the moment. You follow the argument. You nod along. You highlight passages that resonate. But when you close the book, the ideas belong to the author, not to you. You can recognize them but you cannot reconstruct them. You can agree with them but you cannot extend them.
This is epistemologically dangerous. How can you think thoughts that have never been thought before if you are reliant on others’ thinking? How can you discover what the author missed if you never leave the author’s frame?
The Antidote: Writing From Your Own Mind
The solution is to write, not just read. Writing forces you to think your own thoughts in your own words. When you write about something you read, you must:

Extract the core idea from the author’s framing
Translate it into your own conceptual vocabulary
Connect it to what you already believe
Identify where you agree and where you diverge
Find implications the author did not mention

This is not summarizing. Summarizing stays inside the author’s frame. This is synthesizing: taking the raw material of other people’s ideas and building something that is genuinely yours.
Keep Literature Notes Separate
There is an important structural reason to separate your reading notes from your thinking notes. Literature notes capture what the author said, often in the author’s words. They are lightweight records of someone else’s thoughts.
Your permanent notes should be where you do your own thinking. They should be written in your own words, organized by your own concepts, linked to your own ideas. Direct quotes should be rare. The goal is not to build a museum of other people’s insights. The goal is to build a workshop for your own.
When you mix literature notes with thinking notes, it becomes hard to hear yourself think. The author’s voice drowns out yours. Keep them separate. Process literature notes into thinking notes. Then let the literature notes fade into reference material.
The Courage to Think
Kant defined enlightenment as the emergence from self-incurred minority: the inability to use one’s own understanding without direction from another. The cause is not lack of intelligence. It is lack of courage.
It takes courage to think for yourself. It is easier to defer to experts. It is easier to cite authorities. It is easier to collect and summarize and never take a position. But the goal of knowledge work is not to be a faithful curator of other people’s ideas. The goal is to produce new understanding.
Write your own thoughts. Defend them. Revise them when you are wrong. But do not outsource the thinking itself.

notes/dunlosky-2013-study-techniques.title: Dunlosky 2013: Scientifically Ranked Study Techniques
notes/dunlosky-2013-study-techniques.content:
Scientifically Ranked Study Techniques: Dunlosky et al. (2013)
The gold standard meta-analysis of what actually works for studying. Published in Scientific American Mind.

Most Effective (High Utility)




















TechniqueDescriptionOur SystemPractice TestingSelf-testing, flashcards, practice problems, teaching others[[come-studiare/5-step-methodDistributed PracticeStudying over multiple short sessions with sleep between[[come-studiare/recall-schedule
Moderately Effective (Moderate Utility)

























TechniqueDescriptionOur SystemElaborative InterrogationAsking “why” questions to connect new and old material”Why chains” in every noteSelf-ExplanationExplaining problems in your own words while learningStep 2: Read aloud + simplify (“like to a 10-year-old”)Interleaved PracticeMixing different problem types in one session[[come-studiare/index
Least Effective (Low Utility)



































TechniqueWhy It FailsOur SystemHighlighting/UnderliningMotor activity only. Zero cognitive processing.DO NOT DORereadingFluency illusion. Feels easy = not learning.DO NOT DOSummarizationPassive. No retrieval pressure.Write from memory onlyKeyword MnemonicsLimited application. Time-consuming.Use only for very specific casesImagery for TextInconsistent benefits.Use as supplement, not main method

The Bottom Line
Only two techniques have strong scientific evidence: practice testing and distributed practice. Everything else is supplementary.
Our COME STUDIARE method is built on exactly these two:

Practice testing = Step 3 (Close + Recall) + Step 4 (Write from memory)
Distributed practice = The entire Recall Schedule (1h → 3h → sleep → next day → 3 days → 1 week → 1 month)

Everything else: elaboration, interleaving, mind maps, physical anchors: are amplifiers. They make retrieval practice MORE effective. But retrieval practice + spacing IS the engine.

Source: Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K. A., Marsh, E. J., Nathan, M. J., & Willingham, D. T. (2013). Improving Students’ Learning With Effective Learning Techniques: Promising Directions From Cognitive and Educational Psychology. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 14(1), 4-58.

notes/evergreen-notes.title: Evergreen Notes: My System for Thinking in Writing
notes/evergreen-notes.content:
An evergreen note is a note designed to live forever. Not to be filed away and forgotten. To be revisited, revised, and connected: constantly, over years, across every project and course I ever work on.
Why Most Notes Fail
The standard approach fails for one reason: notes are treated as storage. You capture information, organize it by source, and never touch it again. After a year you have a pile of disconnected summaries: Chapter 3 from last semester, Lecture 7 from that one course. None of it adds up to anything.
The problem is not the notes. The problem is the workflow. The work was never about writing notes. The work was always about thinking better.
What Makes a Note Evergreen
An evergreen note has five properties:
Atomic. One note captures exactly one idea. Not one lecture. Not one chapter. One concept. This makes it usable as a building block: you can link to it precisely, revise it without collateral damage, combine it with other atoms to form new thoughts.
Concept-oriented. The note is organized around an idea, not around where the idea came from. A note called “Derivatives measure instantaneous rates of change” can link to physics, economics, and biology examples: regardless of which course introduced the concept.
Densely linked. Links are not decoration. They are the structure. Every note connects to related notes, prerequisite notes, follow-up notes. The web of connections IS the organization: it replaces folders, categories, and hierarchies.
Titled with a claim. The title of an evergreen note is a complete declarative sentence. Not “Photosynthesis.” But “Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy through chlorophyll.” The title tells you the claim. The body defends it.
Written for myself. These notes are not written to teach someone else. They are written so that I can understand the idea six months from now when I have forgotten everything. No background context. No hedging. Raw, honest thinking.
The Lifecycle of an Evergreen Note
Ideas do not start as evergreen notes. They start as whispers: a sentence you read, a connection you noticed, a question that appeared while walking.
These go into an inbox. No structure. No polish. Just capture.
Each morning I process the inbox. Some items get deleted. Some become evergreen notes. The process: write a declarative title, fill in the atomic idea, link it to existing notes. That is it. Five to ten minutes per note.
Then the real work begins: over weeks and months, I pass through old notes while writing new ones. Each pass is an opportunity to revise: sharpen the claim, add a new connection, split a note that grew too large. The note evolves. It gets better every time I touch it.
Why This Compounds
The dense web of links means that writing a new note forces you to re-read old ones. Connecting “Lagrange multipliers” to “gradient descent” means reopening both notes, re-engaging with both ideas. This is spaced repetition: but organic, driven by the work itself, not by a scheduling algorithm.
After a year, the compound effect is dramatic. When I sit down to write an essay or study for an exam, I do not start from zero. I already have dozens of atomic, linked, revised notes on the topic. Writing becomes editing. The hard thinking was done: one five-minute note at a time, every morning.
What Does NOT Get a Note
Not everything is evergreen-worthy. Most of what I encounter: news, casual reading, lecture slides I already understand: gets at most a fleeting highlight.
The filter: if I cannot write a complete declarative sentence as the title, the idea is not ready. It stays in the inbox until it crystallizes. Most ideas never do. That is fine.
Related

understanding-requires-struggle

notes/executable-writing-strategy.title: How to Turn Notes Into Writing: An Executable Strategy
notes/executable-writing-strategy.content:
How to Turn Notes Into Writing: An Executable Strategy
The hardest part of writing is the beginning. You have a vague idea of what you want to say. You open a blank document. The cursor blinks. Your mind goes blank too.
This happens because the traditional writing process asks too much at once. You must simultaneously decide what to say, how to organize it, how to phrase it, and whether it makes sense. That is four hard cognitive tasks competing for the same limited attention. No wonder people get stuck.
There is a better way. If you have been writing good notes all along, writing becomes editing, not composition.
The Note First Pipeline
Here is the strategy:

Write notes continuously while reading and thinking. Each note captures one idea, clearly stated, with a sharp title.
Every time you add a note, link it to an outline. If no outline exists, create one. Let outlines emerge naturally from the connections you discover.
When you feel excited about fleshing out an outline, start there. Your notes are the raw material. The outline is the scaffold.
Write new notes to fill gaps. If a section of the outline has no supporting notes, that tells you exactly what to think about next.
When the outline is well supported, concatenate the note texts. You now have a rough first draft.
Rewrite. The hard part (figuring out what to say) is done. Now you refine.

This works because each step is small enough to feel doable. You are not “writing an essay.” You are “finding notes that seem relevant.” You are not “composing an argument.” You are “connecting two ideas you already understand.” The cognitive load stays manageable.
Why It Works
When you write concept-oriented notes over time, you are effectively writing fragments of future essays without knowing it. Each note is a self-contained piece of thinking. When it comes time to write, you are assembling, not inventing.
This also solves the motivation problem. Starting from a blank page requires enormous activation energy. Starting from a collection of notes you already wrote feels like editing something that mostly exists. The threshold to begin is much lower.
The Emotional Benefit
There is another advantage. When you write the traditional way, every sentence feels precious. Cutting a paragraph feels like losing work. Revising a section feels like admitting failure.
When you write from notes, the emotional stakes are lower. If a paragraph does not fit the current essay, it is still a useful note. It lives in your system, linked to other ideas, ready for future use. Nothing is wasted. Everything compounds.
The Undirected Path
Sometimes you do not know what you want to write about. That is fine. The strategy works in reverse too:

Keep writing notes about whatever interests you.
Let outlines emerge naturally as you notice clusters of related notes.
Eventually, one of those clusters will demand to become something larger.
Follow it.

This is how insight accumulates into output. You do not need to force it. You just need to keep writing good notes and trust that the connections will lead somewhere worth going.
Related

how-i-write-notes
evergreen-notes
note-titles-as-apis
knowledge-work-accretes

notes/goat-me-method.title: The GOAT ME Method: How to Study Based on How Memory Works
notes/goat-me-method.content:
The GOAT ME Method: Study Based on How Memory Works
By u/Salticido: The most upvoted study advice in Reddit history (8,041 points). Based on a university Memory Psychology seminar. This is the foundation that our entire COME STUDIARE system is built on.

Memory works in 3 stages: attention, encoding (storing/associating), and retrieval (remembering). To optimize the final stage, optimize the first two.


GOAT ME: The 6 Principles








































LetterStands ForWhat It MeansGGenerate and TestQuiz yourself. Come up with answers on your own. Even wrong answers help more than reading the right one.OOrganizeOutlines, color coding, timelines. Position becomes meaningful. “CIA FBI KGB” vs “CIAFBIKGBCNNUSABBCUK”AAvoid Illusions of LearningRecognition ≠ Recall. Rereading = fluency illusion. Highlighting = motor activity. Rewriting = hand moves, brain offline.TTake BreaksFrequent short sessions > one cram session. Sleep stores memories permanently.MMatch Learning and Testing ConditionsStudy where you’ll be tested. OR: study under MANY different conditions for long-term retention.EElaborateThink deeply. Make associations. Songs, doodles, stories, visual imagination. Relate to your life.
Additional Key Advice

Take notes BEFORE class. Read textbook chapters ahead of time. In class, add to existing notes instead of frantically writing. Attention is the first step of memory.
Teach to others. You can’t teach what you don’t know. Explaining forces deeper understanding. You’ll discover gaps immediately.
Preview before reading. Skim titles, headings, bold words. Build the mental framework first.

Alternative Acronym: MORBE
Some prefer this ordering: Match conditions → Organize → Recall (combines G+A) → Break → Elaborate.
How GOAT ME Maps to COME STUDIARE

































GOAT MECOME STUDIAREG: Generate and TestStep 3: Close + Recall. Step 4: Write from memory.O: OrganizeSTEP ZERO: 30-second preview. Mind maps. Compare/contrast tables.A: Avoid IllusionsWHY NORMAL STUDYING DOESN’T WORK. Gradient 1-5 Protocol.T: Take Breaks25/5 Pomodoro. Recall schedule (1h, 3h, sleep). Sleep = save button.M: Match ConditionsStudy in exam-like conditions. Also: interleaving = variety of conditions.E: Elaborate”Why” chains. Teach a ghost. Story-ify. Personal examples in every note.

Scientific Validation (from the thread)
PhD Psychology professor (u/lshdevanarchist): Confirmed GOAT ME aligns with Dunlosky et al. (2013) ranking of study techniques:





















EffectivenessTechniquesMost EffectivePractice testing, Distributed practiceModerately EffectiveElaborative interrogation, Self-explanation, Interleaved practiceLeast EffectiveHighlighting/underlining, Rereading, Summarization, Keyword mnemonics, Imagery for text

Source: Dunlosky, Rawson, Marsh, Nathan, Willingham (2013). “What Works, What Doesn’t.” Scientific American Mind, 24(4), 47-53.


This 8,041-point Reddit comment is the single most influential piece of study advice ever shared on the internet. Our entire COME STUDIARE method is an expanded, adapted version of these principles for brains with severe memory challenges.

This site has a built-in interactive knowledge graph: every note is a node, every wikilink is a connection.
How to Access It

Open the site: boluwajioadepojuworkp.github.io/study-notes
Click the graph icon in the sidebar
Or click “Graph View” in the navigation

What You See

Each dot = one note. Bigger dots = more connections.
Each line = a wikilink between two notes.
Hover over a dot to see the note title.
Click a dot to open that note.

notes/highlight-converter.title: Book Highlight Converter
notes/highlight-converter.content:
Book Highlight Converter
A shell script that converts book and article highlights into study notes ready for the knowledge graph.
How it works:
./tools/highlight-to-note.sh highlights.md "Course Name"
This creates a markdown file in the course folder with:

Blockquoted highlights organized by chapter
Timestamps preserved
Frontmatter with course link
Reminder to write proper notes from memory

Why it connects:

Highlights are NOT learning. They create an illusion of knowing.
The converter bridges reading to note-taking. You still must write notes FROM MEMORY after reading highlights.
The workflow: read → highlight → convert → write notes from memory.

Example:
./tools/highlight-to-note.sh tools/example-highlights.md "Machine Learning"

notes/how-i-write-notes.title: How I Write Notes That Actually Think
notes/how-i-write-notes.content:
Most people’s notes are dead. They write them once, close the notebook, and never look again. A year later they have a graveyard of half-remembered thoughts: fuel that was burned and discarded. That is not how I take notes.
My notes think. They evolve. Every time I open an old note, I revise it. Every time I learn something new, I connect it. Over months and years, my notes compound: each one a brick in a structure that gets stronger, not dustier.
What Most People Get Wrong
The typical note-taking workflow looks like this: you read something, you underline it, you maybe write a summary in the margin. If you are diligent, you copy quotes into a notebook organized by source. “Chapter 3 notes.” “Lecture 5.” “Book X highlights.”
This is storage. It is not thinking.
Storage notes answer one question: “Where did I put that information?” Thinking notes answer a different question: “What does this mean, and how does it connect to everything else I know?”
Storage notes are organized by where the information came from. Thinking notes are organized by what the information IS: by concept, not by source.
The Five Principles
After years of experimentation, I have settled on five rules. Every note I write follows them.
1. One Note, One Idea
A note should capture exactly one concept. Not a chapter. Not a lecture. One atomic thought.
This sounds restrictive, but it is the opposite. When a note is atomic, it becomes a building block. You can link it anywhere. You can revise it without touching anything else. You can combine it with other atoms to form new ideas.
If a note is about “the chain rule” AND “implicit differentiation” AND “related rates,” you cannot link to just the chain rule part. You cannot revise the related rates section without touching everything else. The note becomes a blob: hard to use, hard to maintain.
2. Organized by Concept, Not by Source
I do not have a folder called “Calculus Textbook” or “Professor Rossi’s Lectures.” I have notes called “The derivative measures instantaneous rate of change” and “Limits formalize the idea of approaching.”
When you organize by concept, magic happens: you discover that your physics textbook and your calculus textbook are describing the same idea from different angles. The connections reveal themselves because the notes sit next to each other in concept space, even though they came from different courses.
3. Dense Linking Is the Structure
The organization of my notes is not a folder hierarchy. It is a web.
Every note links to other notes. Not as an afterthought: as the primary structure. A note about derivatives links to limits, to tangent lines, to velocity, to optimization. Each of those links back. The knowledge graph IS the organization.
This means I never ask “where should I file this?” I ask “what does this connect to?” The answer to the first question is always “it does not matter.” The answer to the second question IS the work of thinking.
4. Titles Are Claims, Not Topics
A bad note title: “Chain Rule.” A good note title: “The chain rule decomposes complex derivatives into simpler ones.”
When your titles are complete declarative sentences, they do two things. First, they force you to actually understand what you are claiming: you cannot hide behind a vague topic label. Second, they become API endpoints. You can reference “the chain rule decomposes complex derivatives into simpler ones” from any other note, and the title tells you exactly what you are getting.
5. Write for Your Future Self, Not an Audience
When I write notes, I am not trying to be clear for a reader. I am trying to be clear for myself six months from now, when I have forgotten everything.
This means I do not waste time on background context I already know. I do not censor half-formed ideas. I do not add qualifiers and hedges to sound reasonable. The notes are raw thinking: honest, incomplete, evolving.
Publishing them publicly is a separate decision. The thinking comes first.
The Workflow: From Fleeting Thought to Permanent Note
Ideas do not arrive fully formed. They start as whispers.
I keep an inbox: a place to dump thoughts the moment they appear. A sentence I read that struck me. A connection I noticed in the shower. A question I cannot answer yet. No structure, no polish. Just capture.
Once a day: morning, when my mind is clearest: I process the inbox. Some thoughts get discarded. Most get developed: I open a blank note, write a declarative title, fill in the atomic idea, and link it to existing notes.
This morning session is the most important part of my day. It is not “note-taking” in the sense of recording what someone else said. It is thinking in writing: developing my own understanding, making connections, sharpening claims.
Why This Compounds
Here is the most important insight: maintaining notes IS spaced repetition.
Every time I link a new note to an old one, I re-read the old note. Every time I revise a note to incorporate a new insight, I re-engage with the material. The dense web of links forces constant revisiting: naturally, without flashcards, without scheduling algorithms.
After a year of this practice, something remarkable happens. When I sit down to write an essay or prepare for an exam, I do not start from a blank page. I already have fifty atomic notes on the topic. Writing becomes editing: arranging claims into a narrative, filling gaps, connecting threads. The hard thinking was already done, one note at a time.
What I Do NOT Take Notes On
Not everything deserves an evergreen note. Most of what I read is not worth that investment. A news article, a casual blog post, a textbook section I already understand: these get at most a fleeting highlight.
The notes are for ideas I want to keep thinking about. Ideas that are hard. Ideas that connect to other ideas. Ideas I suspect I will need in a year.
The filter is simple: if I cannot write a declarative title for it: one complete sentence that makes a claim: it is not ready to be a note yet. Back to the inbox it goes.
The System in Practice
My tools are deliberately simple. Plain text files. Wikilinks with wikilinks. A graph view that shows me the web of connections.
The technology does not matter much. You could do this with index cards on a desk. What matters is the practice: every day, one or two atomic notes, each linked to something, each titled with a claim, each revised whenever you pass through it again.
After a year, you have not just a collection of notes. You have a thinking environment: a second brain that actually remembers, actually connects, actually thinks.
Related

note-titles-as-apis

knowledge-work-compounds
the-writing-inbox

notes/how-insight-accumulates.title: How Insight Accumulates: From Fleeting Thought to Durable Knowledge
notes/how-insight-accumulates.content:
How Insight Accumulates: From Fleeting Thought to Durable Knowledge
Most thinking is lost. You have an interesting idea while walking. You make a mental note to return to it. You never do. You read something that sparks a connection. You highlight it, close the book, and the connection fades. Day after day, insights emerge and dissolve like morning fog.
This is not inevitable. It is a design failure. The alternative is a system where thinking accumulates instead of evaporating.
Sand Castles and the Tide
Creative work produces a constant stream of partially formed ideas. Most of them are never captured. Of those captured, most are never developed. Of those developed, most sit in isolation, unconnected to anything else.
The result is that each thinking session starts from scratch. You cannot build on yesterday’s insights because yesterday’s insights are gone. You cannot connect last week’s observation to today’s reading because last week’s observation exists only in a notebook you never reopen.
This is the default state of knowledge work. It is also completely unnecessary.
How Accumulation Works
The alternative requires three things:
Capture without friction. When an idea strikes, you need a way to record it immediately, with zero setup, in a place you trust. A pocket notebook. A quick note on your phone. Anything that takes more than five seconds will not be used consistently.
Develop over time. Captured ideas are seeds, not finished products. They need a system that brings them back to your attention periodically. Not as a to-do list (which you will ignore) but as prompts for further thinking. Each time you revisit an idea, you add a layer. After five passes, what started as a fragment becomes a note. After ten passes, it becomes an insight.
Connect relentlessly. An idea in isolation is fragile. An idea connected to ten other ideas is durable. When you link a new note to existing notes, you embed it in a structure that you will encounter again from other angles. The links create the revisitation. The revisitation creates the development.
Why Brainstorming Is a Red Flag
Brainstorming sessions are often a symptom of a broken knowledge system. When you need to schedule a dedicated hour to “think of ideas” about a topic, it means your day-to-day work is not capturing and developing ideas continuously.
In a well-designed system, the ideas are already there. They have been accumulating for weeks or months. The brainstorming session becomes unnecessary because the insight has already emerged through the natural process of writing, linking, and revising.
Brainstorming from a blank slate produces whatever is top of mind. Brainstorming from a dense note network surfaces connections you would never have thought of deliberately. The difference is the difference between guessing and discovering.
Wild Ideas Need Safe Spaces
The most valuable ideas often start as the most fragile. A wild speculation. A hunch that contradicts received wisdom. A connection between two fields that makes no sense at first glance.
If you try to develop such an idea in a single sitting, you will likely fail. The blank page intimidates. The claims feel mushy. The evidence is thin. The natural impulse is to abandon it.
Atomic notes solve this problem. Instead of trying to develop the entire wild idea, write one note about one small facet. That note only has to make sense on its own terms. Tomorrow, write another. Over weeks, the facets accumulate. Connections emerge. The wild idea becomes less wild, not because you forced it, but because you gave it space to grow.
This is how leaps of insight happen. Not in a flash of genius. Through the slow accumulation of small thoughts, each building on the last, until the shape of something new becomes visible. The genius is not in the moment of recognition. The genius is in designing a system that makes the accumulation possible.
Notes Should Surprise You
If your notes never surprise you, you are not linking enough. The value of a note system is not just remembering what you wrote. It is discovering connections you did not consciously make.
When you link a new note to old ones, you sometimes find that two ideas you wrote years apart fit together in a way you never noticed. The connection was latent in the notes but invisible to you until the act of linking made it explicit.
This is combinatorial creativity. New ideas are rarely born from nothing. They emerge when existing ideas collide in unexpected ways. A dense note network makes those collisions more frequent and more surprising.
Related

evergreen-notes
knowledge-work-accretes
knowledge-work-compounds
understanding-requires-struggle

notes/how-learning-works.title: How Learning Actually Works: Brain-Based Methods That Last
notes/how-learning-works.content:
How Learning Actually Works: Brain-Based Methods That Last
Most people study wrong. Not because they are lazy or unintelligent. Because nobody ever taught them how learning actually works in the brain. They use methods that feel productive (rereading, highlighting, copying notes) while avoiding the methods that actually produce durable knowledge (retrieval, elaboration, spaced repetition).
This is a map of what works and why.
How Memory Works
Your memory has three stages: sensory memory (what you perceive in the moment), working memory (what you are actively thinking about, limited to about four chunks), and long-term memory (what you can recall days, months, or years later).
Information moves through all three. Sensory input enters briefly. If you pay attention, it reaches working memory. If you encode it deeply enough, it consolidates into long-term memory. If you never retrieve it, the pathway weakens and fades.
The key principle from neuroscience: neurons that fire together, wire together. When you learn something, the physical structure of your brain changes. New synaptic connections form. Existing ones strengthen. This is neuroplasticity. It means intelligence is not fixed. Your brain adapts to what you demand of it throughout your entire life.
What Actually Works: The Evidence Base
Decades of cognitive psychology research converge on a clear set of findings. These are the methods that work, ranked by the strength of evidence:
1. Retrieval practice (active recall). Close the book. Try to bring the information to mind from memory. Flashcards, self-quizzing, explaining concepts aloud without notes. Every successful retrieval strengthens the memory. Every failed retrieval tells you what to study next.
The optimal ratio for study time: spend 60 percent testing yourself and 40 percent reviewing. Most students do the reverse. They spend 100 percent of their time reviewing and zero percent testing until the exam, when they discover they remember nothing.
2. Spaced repetition. The timing of review matters more than the total amount. Review after one day, then three days, then a week, then a month. Each successful retrieval extends the interval. Cramming produces high performance for 24 hours and zero performance after 48 hours. Spacing produces moderate performance that lasts indefinitely.
3. Elaboration. Take meaningless material and add meaning. Connect new facts to things you already know. Create analogies. Generate your own examples. Explain the concept in your own words. The more connections you create, the more retrieval paths exist to find the memory later.
4. Dual coding. Combine words with visuals. Draw a diagram. Create a sketch. Map the relationships between concepts. Your brain processes verbal and visual information through separate channels. Using both doubles the encoding strength.
5. Interleaving. Mix different topics or problem types in a single session. Do not study three hours of calculus, then three hours of physics. Alternate: 30 minutes of calculus, then 30 minutes of physics, then back. Interleaving feels harder during practice (which is why students resist it) but produces better long-term retention and far better transfer to new problems.
6. Concrete examples. Abstract concepts stick when grounded in specific instances. One example is better than none. Multiple varied examples are better than one. The variation helps you identify what is essential about the concept and what is incidental to the specific example.
Five Learning Myths Debunked
Myth: Learning without effort is possible. Truth: Real learning always requires cognitive effort. Methods that feel easy (rereading, watching videos, listening to lectures) produce weak encoding. If you are not struggling at least a little, you are probably not learning.
Myth: More time equals more learning. Truth: Quality dominates quantity. Two hours of active retrieval produces more durable learning than ten hours of passive reading. The variable is not time spent. The variable is cognitive engagement per unit of time.
Myth: You have a specific learning type (visual, auditory, kinesthetic). Truth: The learning styles theory has no scientific support. Everyone learns better with multimodal input: seeing AND hearing AND doing. Restricting instruction to a supposed type actually reduces learning.
Myth: Intelligence is fixed at birth. Truth: Neuroplasticity means your brain changes throughout life in response to what you do with it. Learning changes brain structure. The more you learn, the more capable of learning you become.
Myth: Rereading is the best way to study. Truth: Rereading produces fluency (the feeling of knowing) without durable memory. It is one of the least effective study methods ever tested.
The Birkenbihl Method: Learning Brand New Topics
Vera F. Birkenbihl developed a five-phase method for learning completely unfamiliar material. It works because it follows how the brain naturally processes new information: from overview to model to terminology to questions to application.
Phase 1: Preparation. Get an overview. Skim the material. Activate prior knowledge: what do you already know about this? Define your learning goal: what specifically do you want to be able to do or explain?
Phase 2: Build a mental model. Translate abstract information into something concrete. Build a physical or drawn model showing how the concepts relate. Use analogies and metaphors. This is the opposite of memorizing: you are building a structure for understanding to live in.
Phase 3: Attach terminology. Now add the formal vocabulary to your model. Label the parts. Connect the technical terms to the concrete images you built in Phase 2. The terms now have something to stick to.
Phase 4: Ask intelligent questions. Systematically interrogate what you have built. Why is this true? How does X connect to Y? What would happen if Z changed? Answer the questions as if explaining to a ten year old. This reveals gaps you did not know you had.
Phase 5: Virtual reality. Mentally simulate using the knowledge in a real situation. Imagine yourself applying it. The brain does not distinguish sharply between vividly imagined experience and real experience. Mental rehearsal strengthens the same neural pathways as physical practice.
The method takes more time upfront than passive reading. It saves vastly more time later because the knowledge actually sticks and requires fewer repetitions.
The Cornell Note Method
Developed at Cornell University in the 1950s, this is the most effective note-taking format for lectures and readings.
Divide your page into three sections:

Right side (two thirds): Main notes during the lecture. Write ideas in your own words, not verbatim transcription.
Left side (one third): After the lecture, add keywords, questions, and cues that reference the main notes.
Bottom (five to seven lines): After the lecture, write a summary from memory without looking at your notes.

The magic is what happens after the lecture. By filling the left column and bottom summary, you process the material a second and third time. By writing the summary from memory, you do retrieval practice. A five-minute summary after each lecture can dramatically reduce total study time before exams.
Do not type your notes. Handwriting engages motor memory and forces you to process and filter information rather than transcribe verbatim. Typed notes tend to be longer and shallower. Handwritten notes tend to be more selective and more deeply processed.
Sketchnotes: Visual Thinking on Paper
Sketchnotes combine text, symbols, and simple drawings. They are not art. They are thinking tools. A circle with a few lines can represent a concept more memorably than a paragraph of text.
You do not need drawing talent. Five basic shapes (circle, square, triangle, line, dot) can represent almost anything. The point is the cognitive processing required to translate an idea from words into images. That translation is where the learning happens.
Combine sketchnotes with Cornell notes: use sketches as your cues in the left column, or draw a concept map at the bottom as your summary.
The Five Step Method for Learning From Texts

Overview. Scan the table of contents, headings, and summaries. Decide if this text is worth deep processing. Not everything is.
Formulate questions. Before reading, write down specific questions you want the text to answer. This primes your attention to find relevant information.
Read with purpose. Read to answer your questions. Skip sections irrelevant to your goals. You do not need to read every word.
Retrieve from memory. Close the text. Write or speak what you learned without looking. This is the single most powerful step and the one most people skip.
Review and reflect. Compare your recall against the text. What did you miss? What did you misunderstand? How does this connect to what you already knew? What are the implications?

The Learning Environment
Your environment shapes your focus more than willpower does.
Clean desk. Visual clutter consumes cognitive resources. Your brain constantly monitors peripheral objects, draining attention you need for learning. Keep only current materials visible.
Fixed location. Study in the same place every time. Your brain associates the location with focused work. Over time, simply sitting there triggers concentration.
Phone out of sight. Not on silent. Not face down. In another room. The mere presence of a smartphone, even turned off, measurably reduces cognitive performance. Your brain devotes resources to not checking it.
Routines. Start at the same time. Use a short ritual (make tea, review plan). Study in blocks (Pomodoro: 25 minutes focused, 5 minutes break). Close the session deliberately. Routines reduce decision fatigue and signal to your brain when to engage and when to rest.
Sleep, Food, Movement
Sleep is when memory consolidates. During deep sleep and REM sleep, the day’s learning transfers from temporary hippocampal storage to durable cortical storage. Less than seven hours of sleep significantly impairs this process. Sleep before learning prepares your brain to encode. Sleep after learning cements what you encoded.
Movement increases BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), a protein that supports the growth and survival of neurons. Even a short walk between study sessions improves subsequent learning. Complex movements (dancing, juggling) enhance neural connectivity more than simple repetitive exercise.
Food. Your brain uses about 20 percent of your body’s energy despite being only 2 percent of your weight. Omega-3 fatty acids support neuron membrane structure. Antioxidants protect neurons from oxidative stress. Dehydration measurably impairs cognitive performance. Light meals before study sessions preserve focus. Heavy meals divert blood flow to digestion.
Exam Preparation That Actually Works
Match the format. Practice in the same format as the exam. If the exam is oral, practice speaking your answers. If the exam is written by hand, practice writing by hand. If you will stand during the exam, practice standing. This is the encoding specificity principle: memory retrieval works best when the retrieval context matches the encoding context.
Simulate the exam. Take a practice test under time pressure. Use past papers. Have someone question you. The simulation should feel slightly uncomfortable. That discomfort is the sign that you are building real readiness rather than false confidence.
Test yourself in proportion 3 to 2. For every two hours of review, spend three hours testing yourself. This ratio produces optimal long-term retention. Most students invert it.
Manage anxiety. Exam anxiety is normal and manageable. Systematic desensitization works: learn a relaxation technique, create a hierarchy of anxiety-provoking situations, and expose yourself gradually while relaxed. Replace catastrophic thoughts with realistic ones. Visualize yourself succeeding despite imperfect knowledge.
Exam anxiety has a physical component. Acute stress can actually enhance learning by mobilizing energy. Chronic stress degrades memory. The goal is not zero stress. The goal is stress that peaks during preparation and settles before the exam.

complete-learning-system

goat-me-method
understanding-requires-struggle

notes/knowledge-work-accretes.title: Knowledge Work Should Compound
notes/knowledge-work-accretes.content:
Knowledge Work Should Compound
Most knowledge work is ephemeral. You spend an hour writing a thoughtful email. It lives in your sent folder. You have a deep conversation with a colleague. The insights fade within days. You solve a hard problem. The solution exists only in your head until the next time you face the same problem and have to solve it again.
This is waste. Not because the work was worthless, but because the output was discarded. The thinking did not accumulate.
The Compound Interest of Thinking
Richard Hamming once asked a colleague how anyone could possibly know as much as John Tukey. The answer: “You would be surprised, Hamming, how much you would know if you worked as hard as he did that many years.”
Knowledge and productivity are like compound interest. Two people of equal ability, one working ten percent more, will produce more than twice the output over a lifetime. The more you know, the more you learn. The more you learn, the more you can do. The more you can do, the more opportunities arise.
But compound interest only works if you keep the principal. If you withdraw your savings every month, there is nothing to compound.
In knowledge work, the “principal” is your past thinking. If you do not capture it, develop it, and connect it, you withdraw everything every day. You start from zero each morning.
Transient Notes Are Not Enough
Most people take notes that serve only the moment. A meeting note that never gets read again. A highlighted passage in a book that fades from memory. A journal entry written in a burst of enthusiasm and never revisited.
These transient notes are like scaffolding. They help you build something in the moment, then get dismantled and discarded. That is fine for some purposes. Sometimes you just need to think through a problem and move on.
But for the ideas that matter repeatedly, transient notes waste the future. Every time you revisit a topic, you should be able to pick up where you left off, not start over.
What Accretion Looks Like
When your notes are written to endure, each session builds on the last. You open a note you wrote six months ago. It is mostly right. You add a new connection you just discovered. You sharpen an argument that was fuzzy. You remove a claim that turned out to be wrong.
Over years, each note becomes a brick in a structure that grows stronger, not dustier. The structure is not a single document. It is a network of densely linked notes, each about one concept, each with a clear title that makes a claim.
When you read a new book, you do not start a new note for that book. You open the existing notes on the concepts the book discusses and integrate the new perspective. The book’s ideas do not sit in a silo. They join a living conversation with everything else you have read and thought.
The Alternative Is Loss
Without accretion, your thinking is like sand castles at the tide line. You build something elaborate, then the water washes it away. Tomorrow you build again, but you cannot build on yesterday’s work because yesterday’s work is gone.
With accretion, your thinking compounds. Every insight becomes a foundation for the next. After years of this practice, your notes contain a depth of interconnected understanding that no single session of thinking could produce.
That is the goal. Not to take better notes. To do better thinking, over time, in a way that builds.
Related

how-i-write-notes
evergreen-notes
understanding-requires-struggle
knowledge-work-compounds

notes/knowledge-work-compounds.title: Knowledge Work Should Compound
notes/knowledge-work-compounds.content:
Most knowledge work evaporates. You write a thoughtful email. You have an insight during a meeting. You solve a tricky problem. And then it is gone: living only in your memory, fading over days, contributing nothing to your future self.
This is not how knowledge work should function. Knowledge work should compound.
The Sand Castle Problem
Day-to-day thinking in creative and intellectual work is like building sand castles at low tide. The work feels real in the moment. But there is no accumulation. Each day you start over, reconstructing context, re-solving problems you already solved last month.
The issue is not that you are not thinking hard enough. The issue is that your thinking has no external structure to accumulate in. Your brain is a leaky bucket. You need a second brain that does not leak.
Compound Interest for Ideas
Richard Hamming, the mathematician, put it plainly: knowledge and productivity work like compound interest. Given two people of equal ability, the one who manages to put in one more hour of real thinking per day will be tremendously more productive over a lifetime. Not a little more. Exponentially more.
The mechanism is simple. Every time you externalize a thought: write it down, make it clear, connect it to other thoughts: you create a permanent asset. Next time that topic comes up, you do not start from zero. You start from where you left off. The new thinking builds on the old thinking. Over years, the difference is not additive. It is multiplicative.
How Notes Enable Compounding
A note that captures an idea and links it to your existing knowledge graph is an investment. You spend ten minutes writing it. In return, you get:

A permanent record of the insight, immune to forgetting
A connection point: other notes can link to it
A revision surface: you will improve it every time you pass through
A building block: it can combine with other notes to form larger ideas

After a year of writing atomic, linked notes every day, you have not just a collection of thoughts. You have a thinking environment: an externalized network of ideas that grows denser and more valuable the more you use it.
What Compounding Looks Like in Practice
You read a book about gradient descent. You write five atomic notes: one for the core algorithm, one for the learning rate, one for the convexity assumption, one for stochastic variants, one connecting it to how rivers find the shortest path downhill.
Six months later, you encounter gradient descent in a completely different context: training a neural network. You open your existing notes. They are already there. You add one link: “Backpropagation computes the gradient that gradient descent needs.” The note updates. The web gets denser.
A year later, you need to explain gradient descent in a project presentation. You open your notes. You have fifteen atomic, linked, revised notes on the topic, enriched by three different courses and two textbooks. You do not write slides from scratch. You arrange claims into a narrative. The hard thinking was done: one five-minute note at a time, over a year.
Why Most People’s Knowledge Does Not Compound
Most knowledge workers have no system for accumulation. Their outputs: emails, code, meeting notes, slide decks: are ephemeral artifacts, not thinking assets. There is no method for capturing the insights embedded in those artifacts and connecting them to a growing body of knowledge.
The result: after ten years in a field, someone might have the same depth of understanding they had after three. The experience accumulated in their intuition, but not in any structured, revisable, connectable form. They got better at their job through repetition, not through compounding insight.
The alternative is not complicated. Write down what you think. Make each thought atomic. Link it to existing thoughts. Revise whenever you pass through. That is the entire system. The rest is just showing up every day.
Related

how-i-write-notes
understanding-requires-struggle

notes/learning-with-ai.title: How to Learn With AI Without Getting Dumber
notes/learning-with-ai.content:
How to Learn With AI Without Getting Dumber
AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude can make you smarter or make you dumber. The difference is not the tool. It is how you use it.
Studies from 2023 and 2024 show that students who use AI to do their homework for them get worse grades, procrastinate more, and report memory loss. Their cognitive abilities atrophy like a muscle that never gets used. But the same studies point to a different path: students who use AI as a Socratic tutor, a thinking partner, and a feedback system perform better and develop deeper understanding.
The question is not “should I use AI to learn?” The question is “am I doing the thinking or is the AI doing it for me?”
The Two Faces of AI
The destructive face: You ask AI a question. It gives you an answer. You read the answer, nod, and move on. You feel like you learned something. You did not. You only recognized something. Recognition feels like understanding in the moment but evaporates within hours. This is the illusion of knowledge, and AI makes it dangerously easy to fall into.
Over time, this creates cognitive atrophy. Why think when the machine thinks for you? Why struggle with a problem when the solution is one prompt away? The brain adapts to what you ask of it. Ask nothing, and it gives nothing back.
The constructive face: You use AI as a trainer, not a crutch. You ask it to question you, not to answer you. You explain concepts in your own words and have the AI check your understanding. You wrestle with problems first, then use AI to find the gaps in your reasoning.
When used this way, AI becomes the most patient tutor imaginable. It is available 24 hours a day. It adapts to your level. It never gets tired of your questions. It can generate infinite practice problems. It can explain the same concept five different ways until one clicks.
The Socratic Method: Ask AI to Question You
The single most powerful technique is to forbid AI from giving you direct answers. Instead, instruct it to use the Socratic method: lead you to discover the answer yourself through well-chosen questions.
The prompt is simple: “Act as a Socratic tutor on [topic]. Do not give me direct answers. Lead me to discover them myself through targeted questions. Start with a simple question and increase complexity based on my responses.”
When you explain something back to the AI, ask it to find gaps in your understanding. Say: “Here is my explanation of [concept] in my own words. Find logical errors, misunderstandings, or gaps and help me fix them.”
This flips the dynamic. Instead of the AI doing the work while you watch passively, you do the work while the AI watches and guides. That is where real learning happens.
The First Think Rule
Before you ask AI anything, think first. Write down your own answer, even if it is wrong. Then ask the AI. Compare your answer to the AI’s. Analyze the differences.
This rule prevents the most common failure mode: outsourcing your thinking before you have even tried. The moment you read an AI answer to a problem you have not attempted is the moment you stop learning and start consuming.
The Four Pillars of AI Learning
Pillar 1: Cognitive activation. Your brain must be actively engaged. Passively reading AI explanations is not learning. Explaining concepts back to the AI in your own words, answering its questions, and solving its problems: that is learning. The AI should make YOU do the work.
Pillar 2: Self-regulated learning. Use AI to reflect on your learning process. Ask it: “Analyze my study approach for [subject]. What am I doing that works? What should I change?” Have it help you build a plan, track progress, and adjust strategies when something is not working.
Pillar 3: Testing and feedback. The most valuable use of AI is testing you, not teaching you. Have it generate practice questions at your level. Have it simulate an oral exam. Ask for brutal honesty: “Give me hard, honest feedback. If something is wrong, tell me clearly what is wrong and how to fix it. Do not sugarcoat.”
AI tends to be too nice. It tells you your work is great when it is mediocre. You must explicitly instruct it to be critical, or you will build false confidence.
Pillar 4: Adaptive difficulty. Tell the AI your target success rate is 70 to 80 percent. If you are getting everything right, the questions are too easy. If you are getting everything wrong, they are too hard. The sweet spot is where you succeed most of the time but still get challenged. This keeps you motivated while ensuring real learning.
AI Across Bloom’s Taxonomy
Bloom’s Taxonomy describes six levels of understanding, from shallow to deep. AI can support every level, but the most value is at the higher levels.
Level 1: Remember. AI generates flashcards and schedules reviews. This is the most basic use. It works but does not require deep thinking.
Level 2: Understand. AI explains complex concepts with analogies from your interests. A sports fan gets sports analogies. A musician gets music analogies. Then you explain it back and the AI checks you.
Level 3: Apply. AI creates practice problems that require you to use what you learned in new situations. Not the same problem with different numbers. A genuinely new situation.
Level 4: Analyze. AI shows you connections between concepts and asks you to identify patterns. “What do these three ideas have in common? How does concept A relate to concept B?”
Level 5: Evaluate. AI acts as a debate opponent. It takes a position and challenges you to respond. It points out weaknesses in your arguments and forces you to strengthen them.
Level 6: Create. AI helps you design projects that synthesize everything you learned. “Design an experiment to test this principle. Build something that uses this knowledge in a novel way.”
Most people never leave Level 1. They use AI to generate summaries and flashcards. They feel productive. They are not. Real learning starts at Level 2 and accelerates at Levels 4 through 6.
The Trap of Surface Level Learning
AI explanations are seductively clear. They make complex topics feel simple. This creates an illusion: you read a clear explanation, you feel like you understand, you move on. But you have not internalized anything. You only followed someone else’s reasoning.
The test: close the AI. Wait a day. Try to explain the concept to someone else. If you cannot do it clearly and correctly, you did not learn it. You only experienced the warmth of recognition without the effort of understanding.
To avoid this, always follow AI explanations with active recall. Explain it back. Answer questions about it. Apply it to a new problem. The AI’s explanation is the starting point, not the destination.
Custom GPTs as Subject Tutors
A powerful technique is creating dedicated AI tutors for specific subjects. A custom GPT for biology uses the Socratic method by default, never gives direct answers, and pushes you up Bloom’s Taxonomy. A custom GPT for programming acts as a code mentor who explains line by line but never writes the solution for you.
The key is the system prompt. It defines the AI’s permanent personality and teaching philosophy. Once set, you cannot accidentally slip into passive consumption mode because the AI will refuse to give you the easy answer.
When AI Actually Makes You Worse
Research is clear on when AI harms learning:

Using AI to complete assignments you did not attempt yourself
Trusting AI answers without verifying against primary sources
Asking for summaries instead of reading the original material
Accepting AI feedback without questioning it
Replacing your own thinking with AI thinking at every step

The pattern is consistent: AI becomes harmful exactly when it replaces cognitive effort. It becomes helpful exactly when it amplifies cognitive effort.
The Implementation Plan

Assess where you are. What are your learning challenges? How much time do you have? What is your goal?
Set up your environment. Save your best prompts as templates. Create custom GPTs for each subject. Plan fixed AI learning sessions.
Do the work. This is harder than just asking for answers. But this is also where the learning happens.
Reflect and improve. Ask AI to analyze your learning process. What worked? What did not? What should you try next?

The rule that ties everything together: think first, ask second, verify always.
Related

understanding-requires-struggle
better-thinking-not-notes

notes/morning-writing-practice.title: The Morning Practice: How I Write Every Day
notes/morning-writing-practice.content:
The most important part of my day happens before noon. Every morning, for two to three hours, I write and revise notes. This is not “taking notes” in the school sense: copying down what someone else said. This is thinking in writing. Developing my own ideas. Making connections. Sharpening claims.
Why Morning
Writing is the hardest cognitive work I do. It demands clarity, focus, and the ability to hold multiple ideas in mind simultaneously. By evening, my cognitive resources are depleted. Morning is when my mind is sharpest.
I can sustain this practice for about two to three hours, with five-minute breaks every twenty-five minutes. After that, the quality of my thinking degrades noticeably. I stop. There is no point producing low-quality notes.
The Ritual
Step 1: Open the writing inbox. My inbox contains undeveloped ideas, half-finished notes, one-line prompts, excerpts from my daily log. I flip through them. If something strikes me: a question I want to answer, a claim I want to sharpen, a connection I want to make: I start writing.
Step 2: Draft an evergreen note. I write an atomic note with a declarative title. I fill in the idea, link it to existing notes, revise it until it stands on its own. This takes anywhere from five to thirty minutes. Some notes get finished in one session. Others get partially developed and returned to the inbox for another day.
Step 3: If the inbox is low, I pull out my pocket memo pad and fill the inbox with new prompts from recent reading and thinking.
Step 4: Ask generative questions about my current projects: What are the most important unknowns? What new ideas am I excited about? I use my daily log as scratch space: a dozen one-liners, emptying everything from my head. Then I develop the ones that seem fruitful.
Step 5: Process the reading backlog. If nothing else works, I open books I have read but not yet processed, review my annotations, and write evergreen notes from insights that still resonate.
Signs Something Is Wrong
If none of these steps produce anything for several days, something is broken. My inputs are not varied enough. I am not giving myself the right kind of creative space. The writing inbox should always feel abundant: a cornucopia of prompts waiting to be developed. If it feels empty, I have stopped paying attention to the world.
Related

the-writing-inbox
knowledge-work-compounds

notes/note-titles-as-apis.title: Note Titles Are Claims, Not Topics
notes/note-titles-as-apis.content:
A bad note title is a noun. “Chain Rule.” “Photosynthesis.” “Memory Systems.” These tell you the topic, not the idea.
A good note title is a complete sentence. A claim. Something you can agree with or argue against. Something that tells you exactly what the note asserts.
Why This Matters
When your titles are declarative sentences, they become API endpoints for your brain. You can reference “The chain rule decomposes complex derivatives into simpler ones” from any other note, and the title alone tells you what you are getting. You do not need to open the note to know whether it is relevant.
This is the difference between a filing system and a thinking system. A filing system labels things. A thinking system makes claims, then defends them.
The Litmus Test
If I cannot write a complete declarative sentence as the title, the idea is not ready. It means one of two things:
My thinking is muddy. I do not actually understand the idea well enough to make a claim about it. The solution is to break it down further: write about the pieces I do understand first.
The note is about too many things. If a note covers three different concepts, no single sentence can title it well. The solution is to split it into three atomic notes, each with its own claim.
Both problems have the same fix: decompose. The title forces decomposition.
How Titles Emerge
I almost never know the title when I start writing. I write the body first: explaining the idea to myself, working through examples, connecting it to other notes. The title usually appears somewhere in the middle of writing, when the core claim crystallizes.
This is a useful signal. When the title emerges easily, the note is working. When I struggle to title something after writing for twenty minutes, the note is broken: too broad, too vague, or not yet understood.
Examples of Good vs Bad Titles
Bad titles (nouns):

Derivatives
Working Memory
Spaced Repetition

Good titles (claims):

The derivative measures instantaneous rate of change
Working memory is limited to roughly four chunks
Spacing reviews over time dramatically improves long-term retention

The good titles tell you the argument. The bad titles tell you the filing cabinet drawer.
Questions Make Good Titles Too
Not every note starts as a claim. Some start as questions: “Why does forgetting follow an exponential curve?” or “How much does sleep quality affect exam performance?”
Question-titles create productive pressure. They demand that the note eventually answer the question: or at least clarify what we still do not know. The goal is to eventually drop the question mark and turn it into a claim. Some questions take years to resolve. That is fine.
The API Analogy
When your notes have sharp, declarative titles, you can compose them. Just as a programmer calls authenticateUser() without reading its implementation, you can reference “The spacing effect makes memories more durable through distributed practice” without re-reading the note. You trust the title. You know what it contains.
Over time, this abstraction layer compounds. You write new notes that reference ten other notes by title, each of which references ten more. The titles become the interface to your entire knowledge graph. You think in claims, not in file names.
Related

how-i-write-notes
evergreen-notes

notes/note-writing-system.title: The Complete Note Writing System
notes/note-writing-system.content:
The Complete Note Writing System
This is the full picture. Everything in one place.
Why This Exists
Most people take notes that go nowhere. They highlight passages, scribble marginalia, fill notebooks, and save bookmarks. After a year, they have a pile of disconnected fragments. The notes did not add up to anything. They were fuel, not structure: written to process the moment, then discarded by time.
The alternative is a note system where thinking accumulates. Each note is a brick. Each link is mortar. Over years, the structure grows taller and stronger, not dustier.
The Core Principles
Evergreen notes. Write notes that are meant to last, not meant to be thrown away. Revise them every time you encounter them. Let them evolve as your understanding evolves. An evergreen note is never finished, only current.
Atomic. One note, one idea. If a note covers three concepts, split it into three. If it covers none, delete it. Atomic notes are small enough to write in under half an hour and specific enough to link precisely.
Concept-oriented. Organize by concept, not by source. A note about memory consolidation belongs with other notes about memory consolidation, regardless of whether the insight came from a textbook, a conversation, or a shower thought. Source-based organization hides connections. Concept-based organization reveals them.
Densely linked. A note with no links is an island. A note with ten links is a hub. Link new notes to old ones. Link old notes back to new ones. When you search for a concept and find surprising connections, you have discovered something you did not know you knew.
Titles as APIs. A note’s title is a promise. It should be a complete phrase making a clear claim. When titles are sharp, they function as handles: you can reference the entire note’s ideas by its title alone. Prefer positive framings: “X enables Y” rather than “X is not Z.”
The Day to Day Practice
Morning writing. Spend the first hours of the day writing and revising notes. This is the highest-leverage activity in the system. Everything else serves this.
Writing inbox. Capture fragments throughout the day: a quote, a question, a half-formed thought. Do not process them now. Drop them in the inbox. Process them later, when you have the mental space to develop each fragment into a proper note.
Reading inbox. A separate place for things you might want to read. Process it regularly. Most items will be discarded. A few will generate notes. The reading inbox is not a library. It is a triage station.
One inbox rule. Do not split inboxes across multiple tools. One writing inbox. One reading inbox. If you have three places to check, you will check none of them.
Phones for capture, not reading. Phones are good for collecting: a quick note, a bookmark, a voice memo. They are bad for deep reading. If you cannot write notes about what you read, you are probably not learning from it. Save serious reading for when you can engage.
How Notes Produce Writing
A note system is not just for thinking. It is a writing engine.
When you need to write something, do not start from a blank page. Open your notes. Shuffle the titles into an outline. Write new notes to fill gaps. Concatenate the note texts. You now have a rough first draft. The hardest part (figuring out what to say) was already done, spread across months of daily note-writing.
This also lowers the emotional stakes of editing. If a paragraph does not fit the current piece, it is still a useful note. It lives in the system, linked to other ideas, ready for future work. Nothing is wasted.
Smooth incremental progress is the key. Each note takes minutes to write but represents solid ground. You can stop at any time and resume later without losing your place. The notes are regular checkpoints in a long thinking process.
The Tools That Matter
Most digital note-taking tools fixate on the wrong thing. They optimize for creating and formatting individual notes while ignoring the sense-making that happens BETWEEN notes. You can have a beautiful, well-tagged, perfectly organized note system that produces zero insight because the notes never interact.
What actually matters: plaintext that will outlast any app, unique identifiers for linking, and you as the human connector who makes the associations. No machine learning algorithm can do this for you. The thinking IS the linking. You cannot automate understanding.
Why Tags Fail
Tags are an ineffective way to organize notes. They feel structured but create false categories. Two notes tagged “psychology” may have nothing meaningful in common. Two notes that genuinely connect may live under different tags and never meet.
Prefer direct links over tags. A link says “this specific idea relates to that specific idea.” A tag says “these ideas share a broad category.” The link is precise. The tag is vague. Precision enables surprise. Vagueness enables clutter.
The History
This approach descends from Niklas Luhmann’s Zettelkasten, a physical slip-box system that produced 70 books and 400 articles over 30 years. The core insight was the same: atomic notes on index cards, densely linked, organized by concept rather than hierarchy, producing combinatorial creativity through structure.
Luhmann wrote that the slip-box “provides combinatorial possibilities which were never planned, never preconceived, or conceived in this way.” The structure itself generates surprise. You follow a link and discover a connection you did not know existed.
The term “evergreen notes” is a modern adaptation. It borrows from Zettelkasten while emphasizing that notes should evolve over time rather than remain frozen. The goal is the same: a system where thinking compounds and insight emerges not from genius but from structure.
Related

evergreen-notes
how-insight-accumulates
executable-writing-strategy

note-titles-as-apis

notes/processing-reading.title: How to Process What You Read
notes/processing-reading.content:
Reading without processing is like eating without digesting. You consumed the material, but you absorbed almost nothing. The solution is a systematic pipeline: collect while reading, then process afterwards into permanent notes.
Phase 1: Collect Lightweight Annotations
While reading, do NOT write polished notes. Switching between reading and writing destroys focus. Instead, capture lightweight annotations:

A couple of words in the margin
A slip of paper with page number + one-line thought
A quick highlight with a one-sentence note

These annotations are not notes. They are reminders: hooks that say “look at this passage again.” They carry just enough information to recreate your mental context from that moment of reading.
For physical books, I use a pocket memo pad. For digital, I use in-app highlights exported to a working note.
Phase 2: Process into Permanent Notes
Days later: ideally in the morning, when my mind is clear: I process the annotations.
1. Cluster the scraps. Spread them out. Patterns emerge. Some ideas cluster together. Some were interesting in the moment but seem shallow now: discard those.
2. Identify the key concepts. Do not use the book’s table of contents. Use your own mental model. What are the concepts that matter to YOU, organized around YOUR existing knowledge?
3. Write broad notes first. For each cluster, write one note that captures the big idea.
4. Write finer-grained notes. Look through individual scraps. Extract specific claims, examples, counterarguments. Each gets its own atomic note.
5. Connect. Search for existing notes that relate to these new ideas. Link them. Merge overlapping notes. Revise old notes to incorporate new insights.
6. Return to the broad notes. Improve them based on what you learned while writing the detailed ones.
What Not to Do
Do NOT try to summarize the book. Do NOT organize notes by the book’s chapter structure. Do NOT keep literature notes mixed with your permanent notes: they are separate things. Literature notes capture someone else’s thoughts in their framework. Permanent notes capture your thoughts in your framework. Keep them apart.
The Reading Inbox
To avoid a proliferation of anxiety-inducing tabs and a terrifying folder of PDFs, I maintain a reading inbox. When I encounter something I might want to read later, I add it to the inbox with zero friction.
Items in the reading inbox get one of four fates: trashed (not worth reading), read deeply (with annotations and processing), read shallowly (skimmed and filed), or deferred to a specific list like “recipes to cook.”
The inbox must be drained regularly. An inbox that accumulates indefinitely stops being useful: it becomes another source of anxiety.
Related

the-writing-inbox
morning-writing-practice

notes/reading-to-notes-pipeline.title: From Reading to Notes: The Full Pipeline
notes/reading-to-notes-pipeline.content:
From Reading to Notes: The Full Pipeline
Reading and note-writing are often treated as separate activities. You read now. You take notes later. This separation is the source of most reading problems.
The effective alternative treats them as a single integrated pipeline: collect while reading, process after reading, integrate into your permanent notes. Each stage has a specific purpose and a specific method.
Stage 1: Lightweight Collection While Reading
Do not try to write polished notes while reading. Switching between reading and composing disrupts both. Your brain uses different modes for consuming and creating. Forcing rapid switching exhausts you and produces shallow results in both modes.
Instead, collect observations in the lightest possible way:

For physical books: tiny notes on a pocket memo pad. One idea per slip. A page number for reference. A dot in the margin at the relevant passage.
For digital texts: copy and paste key excerpts into a single working document in your writing inbox. Do not organize as you go. Do not format. Just collect.
For audiobooks or podcasts: voice memo yourself a few keywords that will jog your memory later.

These annotations are not valuable in themselves. They are reminders. They say “look at this passage later” with just enough context to reconstruct your mental state at the time. They are intentionally incomplete. Their job is to be lightweight enough that you actually do them.
Stage 2: Cluster and Discover Structure
After finishing a chapter or section, look at your collected scraps. Do not try to process them one by one. First, see what patterns emerge.
Spread them out. Group related ones. Notice which ideas keep appearing. Sketch a rough map of how the concepts relate to each other.
The structure you discover does not need to match the text’s structure. In fact, it usually should not. The text is organized for the author’s purposes. Your notes should be organized for yours. What matters to you may be different from what mattered to the author. The concepts central to your thinking may be peripheral to theirs. That is the point.
Stage 3: Write Broad Notes, Then Specific Ones
For each cluster of related observations, write a broad note capturing the big idea. What is the core concept? What claim can you make about it? Write the note as if its title is a promise and its body delivers on that promise.
Then write finer-grained notes for the individual observations within that cluster. Each atomic idea gets its own note. Each note makes one clear claim with supporting detail.
Finally, connect everything. Search your existing notes for related concepts. Link the new notes to the old ones. Merge notes that overlap. Revise old notes that need updating based on what you just learned.
This is an iterative process. You may write a broad note, then discover through writing the specific notes that your understanding of the broad concept has changed. Go back and revise. The loop continues until the notes stabilize.
Stage 4: Let the Literature Notes Fade
Literature notes capture what the author said. They are useful for reference. Keep them around so you can check your sources.
But the permanent value is in your own notes, written in your own words, organized around your own concepts. Once you have extracted the ideas into your note system, the literature notes become secondary. You will rarely need to consult them directly because what matters has already been absorbed and transformed.
The Physical Detail That Matters
There is one implementation detail that dramatically affects whether this pipeline works: handwritten capture beats typed capture for many people.
Writing on paper while reading a book keeps you in the same physical and mental mode. Typing on a screen creates a hard context switch. Your posture changes. Your attention fragments. You start editing instead of capturing. You get pulled into other apps.
A pocket notebook and a pen solve this. Each idea gets its own tiny page. You process the pages later, one at a time, developing each into a proper note. The physical separation between capture and processing is a feature, not a bug.
