notes/sharpening-your-notes.title: Sharpening Your Notes: Titles, Tools, and Conversations
notes/sharpening-your-notes.content:
Sharpening Your Notes: Titles, Tools, and Conversations
These are the finer points of a note-writing practice. The principles that separate a good note system from a great one.
Write Titles That Make Claims
A note’s title should be a complete phrase making a sharp claim. Not a topic label like “Memory” or “Note-taking.” But a specific, declarative statement: “Educational objectives often subvert themselves” or “Evergreen notes permit smooth incremental progress.”
This constraint serves multiple purposes. It forces you to identify the core claim of the note. If you cannot summarize your thinking in a single sharp sentence, your thinking is still muddy. The title acts as a litmus test: write the note, then extract the title. If the title emerges naturally, the note is coherent. If you struggle, the note needs more work.
Questions also work as titles when they get to the core of the matter: “To what extent is exceptional ability heritable?” The long-term goal is to eventually drop the question mark by refactoring into a declarative note once you have an answer.
Prefer positive framings. Instead of “Passive environments cannot offer good metacognitive support,” write “Metacognitive supports require dynamic, participatory environments.” Instead of “Do not read on your phone,” write “Use phones to collect and triage, not to read.” Positive titles foreground the theory of why something matters, making it easier to build on in future notes.
Why Digital Tools Are Missing Something
If you write notes on physical index cards, you naturally arrange them on your desk. Related notes cluster together. Foundational notes sit to the left of dependent ones. As you write a new note, you see the others in your peripheral vision. The spatial arrangement IS the sense-making.
Digital note-taking tools fixate on the note you have open. You cannot see the note you just finished writing, let alone the last four. Backlinks help but they show one note at a time. When you navigate among backlinks, you lose object permanence. The spatial relationships that physical cards make natural are absent.
This is not a small problem. It means digital tools optimize for creating notes while ignoring the primary activity: thinking across notes. You end up with a beautiful collection of well-written individual notes and no way to grasp how they relate to each other except by clicking through links one at a time.
Plaintext is the only defense. Use formats that will outlast any app. Use unique identifiers that work across tools. Accept that you are the human connector in a system that has no single application. The thinking happens in your head, guided by the links you created. No software can automate this part.
Notes as Conversation Amplifiers
If a topic you have thought deeply about comes up in conversation, well-developed notes can increase the bandwidth dramatically. Instead of trying to convey complex ideas through the narrow pipe of spoken words, you can share a link to a note that captures your thinking.
Evergreen notes work particularly well here because they are atomic and self-contained. Short enough to be read on the spot. Polished enough to stand on their own. A one-line summary plus a link can evolve a conversation in realtime in a way that referring someone to a book or article cannot.
When multiple people have written notes on related ideas, something even more interesting happens. Instead of figuratively comparing notes, you can literally compare notes. Each person will have factored the ideas differently. Different angles. Different implications. Different contexts. The one-to-one correspondence fails, and that is the whole point. The differences generate new thinking.
Related

notes/supermemo-20-rules.title: SuperMemo: 20 Rules of Formulating Knowledge
notes/supermemo-20-rules.content:
The 20 Rules of Formulating Knowledge
By Dr. Piotr Wozniak: the inventor of spaced repetition and creator of SuperMemo. These rules are the foundation of HOW to structure what you learn for maximum retention. Published February 1999.

This is THE definitive guide to formulating knowledge for spaced repetition. Every rule below maps directly to our COME STUDIARE method.


The 20 Rules
1. Do not learn if you do not understand
Trying to memorize things you don’t understand is “blind learning”: it takes astronomical time and the knowledge has negligible value.
Our system: Gradient 1 of “I Don’t Understand” Protocol: if you don’t understand, stop. Use the 5-gradient ladder.
2. Learn before you memorize
Build an overall picture BEFORE dismembering it into individual facts. Read the chapter first. Then make individual items.
Our system: STEP ZERO: 30-Second Preview builds the mental shelf before details.
3. Build upon the basics
Simple models first. Basics take a microscopic proportion of your time but each memory lapse on basics costs dearly. You spend 50% of your time repeating just 3-5% of material!
Our system: Always start with the simplest explanation. The note template forces “explain like to a 10-year-old.”
4. Stick to the minimum information principle
The material must be formulated as simply as possible. Simple = easy to remember. Split complex items into sub-items. Example: a paragraph about the Dead Sea → 9 simple Q&A pairs.
Our system: Step 1: BREAK: take only 2 minutes worth. One concept per note. This IS the minimum information principle.
5. Cloze deletion is easy and effective
Fill-in-the-blank format: “Bill …[name] was the second US president to go through impeachment.” Quick, effective, recommended for beginners.
Our system: This is the natural format for our note template: “What it is: (one sentence).” The gaps ARE the retrieval practice.
6. Use imagery
A picture is worth a thousand words. Visual cortex is vastly superior to verbal processing. Use maps, diagrams, mind maps.
Our system: Gradient 5 of “I Don’t Understand”: DRAW it. Rectangle, arrow, stickman. The Quartz graph IS the ultimate imagery.
7. Use mnemonic techniques
Mind maps, peg lists, memory palaces. With training, these become automatic for 1-5% of items. The bottleneck is NOT in quickly memorizing: it’s in retaining for months/years.
Our system: Episodic memory techniques: physical anchors, emotional hooks, location rotation. All are mnemonic techniques.
8. Graphic deletion is as good as cloze deletion
Cover parts of an image and ask: “What is this?” One illustration can generate 10-20 items. Great for anatomy, geography.
Our system: Use the Quartz graph: each node you DON’T link to yet IS a “missing part” of your knowledge picture.
9. Avoid sets
A set is a collection without order (e.g., “What countries belong to the EU?”). Sets are virtually impossible to memorize. Convert to enumerations or ordered lists.
Our system: Never list concepts without structure. Each concept gets its OWN note. Links create the order.
10. Avoid enumerations
Even ordered lists are hard. Use overlapping cloze deletions to break them into pieces. The alphabet example: “What three letters does the alphabet begin with?”
Our system: Pass 2: Cluster: recall 5 sentences in order. This IS overlapping cloze for enumerations.
11. Combat interference
Similar items CONFUSE each other. Memory interference is the #1 cause of forgetting. Make items unambiguous. Eliminate interference as soon as you spot it.
Our system: Implicit Memory: Interleaving: mix problem types. Compare side-by-side. “Spot the difference” tables prevent interference.
12. Optimize wording
Reduce complex sentences to smart, compact maxims. Fewer words = faster learning. The exact wording matters for retrieval speed.
Our system: Step 4: Write ONE sentence. Not a paragraph. ONE sentence. Maximum compression.
13. Refer to other memories
Link new knowledge to existing memories. “Cringing = shamelessly humble and supplicant”: uses words you already know. Builds a coherent, hermetic knowledge structure.
Our system: wikilinks between notes ARE references to other memories. The entire knowledge graph is one big “refer to other memories” system.
14. Personalize and provide examples
Personal references are extremely resistant to interference. “Like the divan at Robert’s parents’ house” is FAR more memorable than a dictionary definition.
Our system: The note template REQUIRES “My example: (a concrete, stupid example).” This is personalization built into every note.
15. Rely on emotional states
Emotions are linked to memories. Vivid, shocking, funny examples stick. Use love, war, your late relative, a celebrity: anything that evokes emotion.
Our system: Dopamine System: chocolate, music, dance. Positive emotions during encoding = stronger memories. The Dopamine Journal builds emotional connection.
16. Context cues simplify wording
Use labels, prefixes, templates to set context instantly. “bioch: GRE” instead of “What does GRE stand for in biochemistry?”: saves milliseconds, prevents wrong brain areas from activating.
Our system: Every note has frontmatter (title, course, date). The course acts as the context cue. [[wikilinks]] ARE context cues.
17. Redundancy does not contradict minimum information
Passive + active recall, reasoning cues, multiple representations, derivation steps: all valid forms of redundancy that STRENGTHEN memory without violating simplicity.
Our system: Step 3 + Step 4: you recall aloud AND write. Two representations. Also: voice + writing = dual coding = productive redundancy.
18. Provide sources
Except for proven facts (2+2=4), always include where knowledge came from. Sources help you update knowledge when it becomes obsolete. Include reliability labels.
Our system: Every note has a “Source” in the template. Every reference note links to the original URL. Date stamping built in via frontmatter.
19. Provide date stamping
Knowledge can be stable (math) or volatile (tech specs). Date stamp items. When knowledge changes, you know what to update.
Our system: Every note has date in frontmatter. Quartz shows “last modified.” The recall schedule IS date stamping for review.
20. Prioritize
You will always face more knowledge than you can master. Prioritize: sources → extracting → transferring → formulating → using forgetting index → learning tools.
Our system: Red) IS prioritization. The recall schedule IS a priority system. Emergency cramming IS last-resort prioritization.

How These Rules Map To Our System





















































































SuperMemo RuleOur Implementation1. Don’t learn without understanding”I Don’t Understand” Protocol (5 gradients)2. Learn before memorizeSTEP ZERO: 30-second preview3. Build upon basics”Explain like to a 10-year-old” in every note4. Minimum informationStep 1: BREAK: 2-minute chunks, one concept per note5. Cloze deletionNote template: “What it is: ___“6. Use imageryGradient 5: DRAW it + Quartz graph view7. Mnemonic techniquesEpisodic memory: physical anchors, emotional hooks8. Graphic deletionGraph view: missing links = “blank spots” to fill9-10. Avoid sets/enumerationsOne note per concept. Links create order.11. Combat interferenceInterleaving + compare/contrast tables12. Optimize wordingStep 4: ONE sentence only13. Refer to other memorieswikilinks throughout all notes14. Personalize”My example” in every note template15. Emotional statesDopamine System + Dopamine Journal16. Context cuesFrontmatter (course, date, tags)17. Redundancy (good kind)Voice + writing = dual coding18. Provide sourcesSource field in every note19. Date stampingFrontmatter date + Quartz last-modified20. PrioritizeGreen/Yellow/Red protocol + recall schedule

The Big Insight
Wozniak’s first 16 rules are ALL about making knowledge SIMPLE. Our entire COME STUDIARE method is built on exactly this principle:

Small chunks (2 min)
Simple words (“like to a 10-year-old”)
One sentence per concept
Draw it if words fail
Link to what you know


Every rule above is already built into our system. Wozniak invented spaced repetition. We built a method that applies ALL 20 rules automatically.


Source: supermemo.com/en/blog/twenty-rules-of-formulating-knowledge: Dr. Piotr Wozniak, February 1999. The single most influential article on knowledge formulation in the history of learning science.

notes/the-writing-inbox.title: The Writing Inbox: Capture Now, Think Later
notes/the-writing-inbox.content:
Ideas do not arrive on schedule. They appear in the shower, during a lecture, while walking, in the middle of a conversation. If you do not capture them immediately, they vanish.
But you cannot stop everything to develop each idea into a proper note. You would never get anything done.
The solution is a writing inbox: a frictionless capture zone where thoughts land while they are hot, to be processed later when your mind is clear.
How the Inbox Works
The inbox is deliberately unstructured. A single line is enough: “Connection between gradient descent and how rivers find the shortest path downhill?” No explanation needed. No context. Just enough to reconstruct the thought later.
I keep one inbox for everything:

Ideas from reading (page number + one-line thought)
Questions that appeared while studying
Connections I noticed between concepts
Half-formed project ideas
Interesting phrases or analogies

The inbox is not organized. It is a queue. The only rule: every item must be short enough to write in thirty seconds.
Why Separation Matters
Mixing transient thoughts with permanent notes is deadly. If your inbox items sit in the same folder as your polished evergreen notes, two things happen:
First, the polished notes feel less polished. They are surrounded by mess. The quality distinction blurs.
Second, and worse, the transient thoughts never get processed. They hide among the permanent notes, invisible, forgotten. The inbox becomes a graveyard of good intentions.
The physical or visual separation: a different folder, a different color, a different app: forces the question: “Have I processed this yet?” Every item in the inbox is implicitly asking for attention. Every item outside it has been handled.
The Morning Processing Ritual
The inbox only works if you drain it regularly. An inbox that accumulates without processing is worse than no inbox at all: it becomes a source of guilt rather than clarity.
Each morning, I spend twenty to thirty minutes processing the inbox:

Open the oldest items first
For each: does this still seem interesting?
If no: delete it. Most ideas are not worth keeping.
If yes: write an evergreen note. Atomic. Titled with a claim. Linked to existing notes.
If “yes but not ready”: leave it in the inbox for another pass

Some items cycle through the inbox multiple times. An idea might sit for weeks, getting a little more development each morning, until it finally crystallizes into a claim worth writing about. That is fine. The inbox is a nursery, not a factory.
What I Capture (and What I Do Not)
I capture ideas that have tension: something surprising, confusing, contradictory, or oddly connected. The feeling of “hmm, that is strange” is the most reliable signal.
I do not capture:

Facts I can easily look up
Reminders (those go in a task list, not the inbox)
Things I “should” read later (those go in a separate reading queue)
Vague feelings without a concrete observation attached

The filter is simple: will I still care about this next week? If I am not sure, it goes in the inbox. The morning processing will answer the question.
Related

writing-from-reading
daily-log
morning-writing-practice

notes/understanding-requires-struggle.title: Why Understanding Requires Struggle
notes/understanding-requires-struggle.content:
You cannot understand something by reading it. You cannot understand something by hearing it explained. Understanding is not a transfer: it is a construction. You have to build it yourself, from the inside, through effort.
The Transmission Myth
The dominant metaphor for learning is transmission. A teacher “transmits” knowledge to students. A book “conveys” information to readers. This metaphor is wrong.
What actually happens when you read: your eyes scan words. Your brain recognizes patterns. You feel a sense of familiarity: “yes, I get this.” But familiarity is not understanding. Familiarity is the feeling of recognition when someone else does the thinking for you.
Schopenhauer warned about this two hundred years ago: “When we read, someone else thinks for us. We merely repeat his mental process. Accordingly, in reading we are for the most part absolved of the work of thinking.”
The solution is not to read less. The solution is to think alongside the reading: to wrestle with the ideas, not just receive them.
What Effortful Engagement Looks Like
Real understanding requires asking questions the text does not answer:

Where does this apply, and where does it not?
What are the hidden assumptions?
Whose perspective is represented here?
What does this idea refute or contradict?
How would I explain this to someone who disagrees?
What would happen if the opposite were true?

These questions are not optional extras. They ARE the work of understanding. Without them, you have not understood: you have only recognized.
Grant Sanderson, the creator of 3Blue1Brown, put it memorably: “You need to make eye contact with the idea.” You cannot absorb it through peripheral vision. You have to stare at it directly, turn it over, test its edges.
Writing Forces Understanding
The most reliable way to force yourself to engage with an idea is to write about it. Not summarize it. Explain it. In your own words. With your own examples. Connected to your own existing knowledge.
Writing exposes the gaps. When you try to explain something and find yourself reaching for the author’s exact phrasing, you have discovered that you did not actually understand: you only recognized. When you cannot think of a concrete example, you have discovered that the idea is still abstract and untethered in your mind. When you cannot connect it to anything else you know, you have discovered that it sits in isolation, vulnerable to forgetting.
These discoveries are gifts. They tell you exactly where to direct your effort. Every gap you find and fill makes the understanding stronger.
The Collector’s Trap
Collecting feels like understanding. Saving a PDF, bookmarking a webpage, highlighting a passage: these actions produce a sensation of progress without any actual cognitive engagement.
This is the collector’s fallacy. Having a text at hand does nothing to increase your knowledge. The information is there, but it is not in you. You have outsourced the storage to a system that cannot think.
The antidote is to process what you collect. Short iteration cycles: read something, write about it, link it to your existing notes. Do not let the backlog of unprocessed material grow. An inbox of fifty unread PDFs is not a resource: it is a monument to avoidance.
The Stakes
If understanding required only exposure, every student who attended every lecture would ace every exam. They do not. The difference between students who understand and students who only recognize is effort: the deliberate, uncomfortable work of grappling with ideas until they yield.
This is uncomfortable because it reveals what you do not know. It is easier to highlight a sentence and feel accomplished. It is harder to close the book and ask: what did I just learn, in my own words?
Choose the harder path. It is the only one that leads anywhere.
Related

evergreen-notes
knowledge-work-compounds
how-i-write-notes

notes/write-to-internalize.title: Write About What You Read to Internalize Deeply
notes/write-to-internalize.content:
Write About What You Read to Internalize Deeply
Highlighting a passage feels like learning. It is not. Underlining a sentence requires no cognitive effort beyond moving your hand. Writing notes in the margins is slightly better, but still shallow: you react to what the author said without synthesizing it into your own thinking.
The only way to truly internalize something you read is to write about it, in your own words, from memory, connecting it to what you already know.
Why Surface Level Interaction Fails
When you only highlight or annotate, you stay in the author’s frame. You identify what looks important but you do not process why it matters. You collect quotes but you do not integrate them. A year later, you open the book, see your highlights, and feel nothing. The ideas did not stick because you never did the work of making them yours.
Writing changes this. When you sit down to write a note about something you read, you must:

Recall the key idea without looking at the text (retrieval practice)
Explain it in your own words (elaborative encoding)
Connect it to other ideas you have already written about (integration)
Identify what is missing or unclear (metacognitive check)

This is not passive consumption. This is active thinking. And active thinking is what makes ideas durable.
The Two Layer System
Effective reading notes use two layers:
Layer 1: Lightweight collection. While reading, mark passages that seem important. Write brief, fragmented thoughts in the margins or in a quick capture system. Do not try to write polished notes while reading: switching between reading and composing disrupts both.
Layer 2: Deep processing. After finishing a chapter or section, return to your marks and fragments. For each one that still seems valuable, write a proper note. Explain the idea clearly. Connect it to your existing notes. Argue with it if you disagree. Extend it if you see implications the author missed.
Most people stop at Layer 1. They collect. They never process. The collection grows into an overwhelming pile that feels useful but never actually feeds back into their thinking.
Not Every Text Deserves This
Some things you read are not worth deep processing. Most articles, most social media posts, most light nonfiction: read them, enjoy them, move on. Spending two hours writing notes about a blog post that took twenty minutes to read is a poor investment.
Save deep processing for texts that genuinely advance your thinking. A book that challenges your assumptions. A paper that introduces a new framework. An essay that crystallizes something you have been circling. These are worth the effort because they change how you think, not just what you momentarily know.
The Test
Here is the test of whether you have internalized something you read: close the book. Wait a day. Open a blank document. Write down what mattered, why it mattered, and how it connects to what you already believed.
If you cannot do this, you did not learn it. You only recognized it. Recognition feels like understanding in the moment but vanishes when the book is closed. Only recall plus synthesis produces durable knowledge.
Related

writing-from-reading
processing-reading
understanding-requires-struggle
how-i-write-notes

notes/writing-from-reading.title: How I Turn Reading Into Permanent Notes
notes/writing-from-reading.content:
Reading without writing is entertainment. You feel smarter, but nothing sticks. The understanding fades within days.
Writing about what you read is how you actually internalize it. Not highlighting. Not copying quotes. Writing in your own words: making claims, connecting to existing knowledge, extracting the core idea and rebuilding it from scratch.
Why Highlighting Does Not Work
Highlighting feels productive. It is not. When you drag a yellow line across a sentence, your brain registers the motor activity as “work done” and disengages. You have outsourced the thinking to a color.
Even worse, highlights do not accumulate. A year later, you have fifty books with yellow lines in them. None of it adds up to anything. The insights are trapped inside the books, inaccessible, disconnected from each other.
The alternative: read with a pen (or a notes app open). When something strikes you, capture it briefly. Then, later, write a proper note about it: in your own words, linked to your existing notes.
The Two-Phase Reading Workflow
Phase 1: Collect (While Reading)
Do not interrupt your reading flow to write polished notes. That is too heavy. Instead, capture lightweight annotations:

A sentence that made you stop and think
A connection you noticed to something you already know
A question the text raised but did not answer
A disagreement: “I think this is wrong because…”

Each annotation gets a page number. That is it. No structure, no polish. The goal is to keep reading while capturing enough to reconstruct your thoughts later.
I use a pocket memo pad for physical books and a writing inbox for digital reading. One idea per note. Dozen of tiny scraps.
Phase 2: Process (After Reading)
Days or weeks later: preferably in the morning, when my mind is clear: I process the pile.
I spread the annotations out. Patterns emerge. Some ideas cluster together. Some were interesting in the moment but seem shallow now: those get discarded. The ones worth keeping become evergreen notes.
For each lasting insight, I write one atomic note:

A declarative title making the claim
The idea, explained in my own words
Links to related notes I already have
The book/page reference tucked at the bottom (for my own records)

The key: I do NOT try to summarize the book. I extract the concepts that matter to ME, organized around MY existing knowledge, not around the book’s table of contents.
What Gets a Note and What Does Not
Not every book deserves this treatment. Most of what I read is not worth the hours it takes to write good notes. A news article? No. A casual blog post? Probably not. A textbook chapter I already understand? Just a few quick highlights.
The filter: if I cannot write a sharp declarative title for the idea, it is not ready. If weeks pass and I never feel the urge to write about something I highlighted, it was not important enough.
I write notes for maybe 5% of what I read. That 5% is what compounds.
Why This Compounds
When I write a note about a concept from one book, and six months later I read another book about the same concept, I do not start from scratch. I open my existing note. I see what I already understood. I compare. The new book might confirm, contradict, extend, or reframe. I update the note.
After a few years, that note has been revised ten times, enriched by five different sources, linked to twenty other notes. It is no longer a “book summary.” It is a thinking artifact: denser and more precise than any single source.
Related

the-writing-inbox
